docs.title: Macro Flux
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 8 8AM to May 8 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Ceasefire hopes suffered a fresh blow as the UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles, forcing Trump to insist the truce still holds while oil prices surged and Asian equities dropped. Trump set a July 4 deadline for the EU to ratify its trade deal or face “much higher” levies, and he invited Boeing and Mastercard CEOs to join his Beijing summit next week, injecting two‑way risk into the trade outlook. Prime Minister Starmer defied heavy local election losses and said he would stay on, triggering a relief rally in gilts and sterling. Japan’s wage data kept a June rate rise on the table as intervention speculation simmered. The cross‑currents harden a stagflationary impulse in which energy‑supply fear, trade brinkmanship, and political fragility converge on sustained volatility.

docs.title: 📌 UAE Intercepts Iranian Drones as Ceasefire Strain Deepens
docs.text:
Fact The UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles as tensions escalated in the Strait of Hormuz, even as Donald Trump insisted the ceasefire was still in effect (FT). The US military said it carried out retaliatory strikes against Iran after what it described as unprovoked attacks on its warships transiting the strait, while Iran accused the US of breaching the ceasefire and attacked American destroyers (Reuters). Brent crude rose sharply as both sides exchanged fire in the critical waterway (Al Jazeera). Trump said the ceasefire still holds but warned Iran to “sign the agreement fast” (Al Jazeera). The US also struck Iran’s Qeshm port and Bandar Abbas (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: UAE interceptions physically widen the conflict radius, making a near‑term reopening of Hormuz less credible and entrenching the oil‑supply risk premium that feeds into global inflation.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to flat/neutral if a verified US‑Iran de‑escalation communiqué and unimpeded Project Freedom convoy transit materialise within 24 hours.

docs.title: 📌 Trump Readies Slimmed‑Down CEO Delegation for Beijing Summit
docs.text:
Fact The Trump administration is inviting American CEOs from Boeing, Mastercard, Citigroup and other major corporations to join his delegation to China next week, according to multiple sources (SCMP_Econ). Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg said in April that the company was counting on the Trump administration to help unlock a long‑awaited major order from China (SCMP_Econ). The White House considered inviting about a dozen business leaders, compared with the 29 high‑profile executives on Trump’s last visit in 2017, Reuters reported (SCMP_Econ). Global soybean participants are awaiting the US‑China meeting with mixed expectations (SP_Global_Commodities). Trump overnight also gave the EU a July 4 deadline to ratify its trade agreement or face “much higher” tariffs (CNBC).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The China summit holds a binary catalyst for trade sentiment; a Boeing deal would signal that Sino‑US tensions are being managed despite the Iran war, while a failure would amplify protectionist fears and add to global growth headwinds.
  • Tactical Trade: Favours a tactical easing of CNH downside hedges ahead of the summit; PIVOT to add downside CNH protection if the EU rejects the July 4 deadline and the Beijing meeting fails to produce a concrete commercial outcome.

docs.title: 📌 Starmer Defies Heavy Losses, Gilts Rally on Political Survival
docs.text:
Fact UK government bonds rallied as Prime Minister Keir Starmer said he would stay as Labour leader despite the party’s heavy losses in local elections (BBG). Sterling also rose as Starmer vowed to fight on (Reuters). The populist Reform UK party made early gains, punishing Labour in former strongholds (Al Jazeera). The result deepens doubts over Starmer’s ability to govern but removes the immediate threat of a leadership vacuum (SCMP_Econ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Political continuity removes a near‑term tail risk for UK assets, but the surging Reform vote signals that fiscal populism and a harder line on trade could gain traction, complicating the medium‑term policy outlook.
  • Tactical Trade: Fading extreme gilt underperformance is warranted in the very short term; however, PIVOT to neutral if polling suggests Reform could emerge as a credible governing alternative.

docs.title: 📌 Japanese Wage Data Keeps June Hike in Play as Intervention Talk Swirls
docs.text:
Fact March wage growth in Japan undershot expectations but pressure to tighten is still on, supportive of a June rate rise (FT). Japan’s Ministry of Finance has intervened in the yen over the country’s Golden Week holiday, though markets are testing Tokyo’s resolve (CNBC). The service sector grew at the slowest pace in 11 months in April, according to the final au Jibun Bank PMI (BusinessTimes).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A June rate hike would put further upward pressure on JGB yields and narrow the rate gap with the US, providing fundamental support for the yen beyond intervention; a dovish tilt would trigger a fresh sell‑off.
  • Tactical Trade: Aggressively scaling into long JPY positions ahead of the June meeting once intervention‑driven volatility subsides.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• German industrial production unexpectedly fell by 1.2% in March, the second consecutive decline, adding to worries for Europe’s largest economy as it grapples with the Iran war (BBG).
  • Global food prices climbed to their highest level in more than three years as the Iran war disrupted supply chains (BBG).
  • The Philippines vowed a rapid ramp‑up in government spending starting this month to revive an economy hit by a sharp slowdown in growth and surging consumer prices (BBG).
  • Japan’s service sector growth slowed to an 11‑month low in April, with the au Jibun Bank Services PMI coming in at 52.2 (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• ECB Vice President Guindos said the status of the Strait of Hormuz will be “key” for the June monetary policy meeting (BBG).
  • Bank of England minutes from the May 7 Market Participants Group meeting highlighted that financial market participants expect persistent inflationary pressure from the energy shock (BOE_News).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• The UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles, marking the first direct third‑party kinetic engagement in the current crisis (FT).
  • Trump gave the EU until July 4 to ratify its trade agreement or face “much higher” tariffs (CNBC).
  • Southeast Asian leaders at the ASEAN summit called for deeper energy cooperation and a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz as the fuel crisis dominated the agenda (Al Jazeera).
  • Global perceptions of the US have fallen below Russia under Trump, a new international survey finds (Reuters).
  • China confirmed it helped Pakistan’s air force with on‑site technical support during last year’s war with India (SCMP_China).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Brent crude jumped after the US and Iran exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz, with oil prices rising on the back of renewed supply‑disruption fears (Al Jazeera).
  • Global sulfur prices have surged due to Middle East disruptions and a Chinese export ban (SP_Global_Commodities).
  • A primary aluminum supply crunch is boosting scrap demand and prices (SP_Global_Commodities).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text: • Asia‑Pacific markets mostly fell as renewed US‑Iran clashes kept investors on edge, with Hong Kong’s Hang Seng sliding 232 points and chip stocks retreating (CNBC, HKEJ).

docs.title: 2026 05 04 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 4 8AM to May 4 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Intraday price action was dominated by a violent two-way swing in crude and Asian currencies after Iran claimed to have hit a US warship with missiles in the Strait of Hormuz—a claim swiftly denied by Washington—while a tanker attack off Fujairah kept the physical supply risk firmly on the table. Brent spiked intraday before paring gains on the US denial, while KRW and KOSPI rallied to new records on AI optimism, decoupling from the geopolitical noise as the BOK signalled an eventual rate hike. China's unprecedented activation of "Blocking Rules" against US sanctions on oil refiners is now the dominant macro overhang for CNH and the broader decoupling narrative. Across markets, tech earnings momentum is grinding Asian equities ex-Japan to all-time highs, while the ECB confirmed a June rate hike is "all but inevitable," cementing a hawkish global central bank pivot.

docs.title: 📌 Hormuz Live Fire: Iran Claims Missile Strike on US Warship; US Denies, Tanker Hit
docs.text:
Fact Iranian news agency says two missiles hit a US warship trying to enter the Strait of Hormuz (Reuters). The US denied an Iranian report that the country had struck an American naval vessel with missiles, Axios reported citing a senior official (BBG 1). A tanker was hit by unknown projectiles off UAE's Fujairah, UKMTO says (Reuters). The US military supports the launch of Project Freedom in the Strait of Hormuz beginning Monday (US_CENTCOM). The Iranian military warned the US of attacks over the Hormuz mission (Al Jazeera 1). Iran's Revolutionary Guards unveiled a new map of the Strait of Hormuz under their control (Reuters). The US Hormuz plan leaves shipowners guessing as attacks persist (BBG 2). Oil prices were flat as Trump's plan for the Strait of Hormuz failed to calm the market (Al Jazeera 2). OPEC+ agreed a third oil output quota hike since the Hormuz closure, announcing a 188,000 barrels-per-day output increase in its first meeting without the UAE (CNBC). Some 32 volcanic earthquakes, including 25 volcanic tremors, were reported as more than 300 families were evacuated after massive ash from Mayon volcano blanketed towns in the Philippines (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The live-fire denial cycle keeps Brent in a $5-7 risk premium range, with the market pricing a high probability of further attacks but no full closure.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent calendar spreads vs flat price; PIVOT to neutral if Project Freedom transits one vessel without incident within 48 hours.

docs.title: 📌 China Activates "Blocking Rules" vs US Sanctions: Showdown Over Banks Looms
docs.text:
Fact China ordered companies to ignore US sanctions against five oil refiners accused of buying Iranian crude, an unprecedented pushback against American financial measures less than two weeks before a presidential summit (SCMP). China’s Ministry of Commerce said the US action "violates international law and the basic norms of international relations," covering a refinery in Dalian owned by Hengli Petrochemical and four independent "teapot" refineries (SCMP). China’s rare defiance of US sanctions sparks a showdown over banks (BBG 1). An unfinished Iran war could give Xi the upper hand in Trump talks, sources say (CNN 1). China and Indonesia launched a new cross-border QR payment system, marking the latest step in Beijing’s drive to build a regional digital payments network (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The blocking rules are a high-stakes bargaining chip ahead of the Xi-Trump summit, with real implementation uncertainty keeping UST yield bid and CNH capped.
  • Tactical Trade: Short CNH/CNY via 1m NDF; PIVOT to flat if US Treasury Secretary Bessent signals a waiver or delay in secondary sanctions enforcement.

docs.title: 📌 BOK Signals Hawkish Turn; KOSPI & Asian Tech Record Rally Decouples
docs.text:
Fact Top Bank of Korea official says it's time to consider a rate hike (BusinessTimes). South Korean stocks hit a fresh record, building on a historic monthly rally in April (CNBC). Asian stocks outside of Japan hit a record high as tech rallied, with AI chipmakers in Korea and Taiwan driving the move (BBG 1). SK Hynix shares rallied 13% after US tech firms signaled strong AI spending plans (Reuters). Hong Kong's Exchange Fund gained HK$34.5 billion (US$4.4 billion) in the first quarter, its smallest gain in five quarters (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The BOK hawkish signal will cap KRW downside but reinforce the tech-liquidity tightening tension, favoring a funded short KRW vs long KOSPI trade.
  • Tactical Trade: Long KOSPI / Short KRW via futures; PIVOT to flat KRW if BOK delivers a hold with explicit hawkish guidance at the next meeting.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• Turkish annual inflation rose more than expected in April to 32.4%, as price pressures from the Iran war continued to ripple through the economy (BBG).
  • Indonesia’s April inflation eased to 2.42%, while its March trade surplus expanded to US$3.32 billion (BusinessTimes).
  • A gauge measuring South African manufacturer sentiment rose in April to its best level in two years, driven by business activity and new sales orders, potentially reflecting front-loading ahead of price increases due to the Iran war (BBG).
  • Vietnam’s inflation hits a six-year high, trade gap at a 30-year peak (BusinessTimes).
  • A court in China has ruled it illegal for a company to terminate an employee on the grounds that an AI replacement would be cheaper (SCMP).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• ECB (Kazimir) — An ECB rate hike in June is "all but inevitable," the governing council member said (BBG 1).
  • ECB (Villeroy) — The ECB's Villeroy urged caution and readiness on rate hikes (BBG 2).
  • ECB (De Guindos) — ECB's de Guindos is scheduled to speak at 8:30 PM HKT (TradingEconomics).
  • The ECB Survey of Professional Forecasters for Q2 2026 showed the euro-zone inflation jump seen as temporary (BBG, ECB).
  • The yen briefly spiked, leaving traders on high alert for intervention after last week's move (BBG). Goldman Sachs estimates Japan spent ¥5 trillion on intervention last week and has capacity for another 30 rounds (HKEJ).
  • HKMA — Hong Kong Monetary Authority CEO Eddie Yue said there is no intention to change the Hong Kong dollar peg system, and a basket peg would be operationally difficult (HKEJ).
  • The ASEAN+3 Finance Ministers' and Central Bank Governors' Meeting issued a joint statement, though details were not available in this window (MAS).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• The EU seeks US trade talks but is ready to respond to Trump's tariff threat, a top official said (BBG 1). Trump is planning a 25% tariff on European autos (BBG 2).
  • Singapore and New Zealand signed a legally binding bilateral pact to keep essential supplies—including food, fuel, and healthcare products—flowing during crises (SCMP, FT).
  • Japan's PM says the Iran war oil crisis is having an "enormous impact" on the Asia Pacific region (Al Jazeera).
  • Australia and Japan signed agreements on energy and critical minerals supplies (Reuters).
  • The US has evacuated 22 crew members from a seized Iranian ship (MV Touska) to Pakistan (Reuters).
  • NATO chief Rutte says Europeans have "gotten the message" from Trump on defence spending (Reuters).
  • The US calls Taiwan a "trusted and capable partner," praising its ties with Eswatini (Reuters).
  • North Korean women's football club Naegohyang FC will play in the South on May 20 in the semifinal of the Women's Asian Champions League (Al Jazeera).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Oil steadied as traders questioned the workability of Trump's plan for the Strait of Hormuz, with a tanker reportedly hit in the waterway (BBG).
  • Oil slipped initially after Trump said the US will assist stranded ships, but the plan left shipowners guessing as attacks persist (WSJ). More than double the gas stuck in Hormuz is wasted each year, the IEA says (FT).
  • Gold was steady with focus on the US Hormuz plan and Iran peace talks (BusinessTimes).
  • UAE fertiliser giant Fertiglobe has resorted to trucks to move product out of the Gulf, avoiding the Strait of Hormuz (FT).
  • Saudi Arabia is touting a new use for its Neom port after the Iran war, focusing on Red Sea infrastructure (FT).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• Asian stocks ex-Japan hit a record high as AI chipmakers in Korea and Taiwan rallied (BBG). KOSPI closed at a record high, up 5.1% (HKEJ). Hang Seng Index rose 319 points to close back above 26,000 (HKEJ 1). South Korean stocks hit a fresh record, building on a historic monthly rally in April (CNBC).
  • European stocks tumbled as intensifying rhetoric between the US and Iran on Hormuz pushed up oil prices (BBG 2).
  • Morgan Stanley sees tech earnings eclipsing the Iran war for stocks (BBG). Morgan Stanley upgraded its forecast for Hong Kong home prices to a 12% increase this year (SCMP).
  • GameStop offered to buy eBay for about US$56 billion (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 2026 05 05 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 5 8AM to May 5 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

The afternoon session saw a clear risk-off tilt in Asia, led by a retreat in Hong Kong equities as HSBC missed profit estimates on a fraud-related charge and provisions for the Iran war, with the Hang Seng Index shedding over 300 points. Oil prices eased from earlier highs as a Maersk vessel successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz under US escort, but the ceasefire remains fragile after Iran struck an Emirati oil port and a South Korean-operated ship. South Korea rebuffed Trump’s call to join “Project Freedom,” while the EU sounded out industry on a new trade weapon targeting Chinese overcapacity. The cross-market thread: the dollar held firm on haven demand, Brent stayed above $110, and Asian FX—particularly the rupiah and rupee—came under renewed pressure.

docs.title: 📌 Hong Kong: HSBC Misses, HSI Slides on Risk-Off
docs.text:
Fact Hong Kong’s benchmark Hang Seng Index fell as much as 1.6% in afternoon trading, on track for its biggest drop in three weeks, dragged by HSBC Holdings Plc after the lender missed first-quarter earnings estimates, booking a fraud-related charge and provisions for the Iran war (BBG 1, 2). HSBC reported a first-quarter pre-tax profit of $9.4 billion, marginally missing analysts' estimates, with higher expected credit losses (CNBC). The bank’s net profit was broadly flat at $6.94 billion, missing the $7.07 billion consensus, as “lower interest rates and provisions linked to the Middle East conflict offset solid growth in its wealth management business” (SCMP). HSBC’s CFO said there is still confidence in achieving a full-year RoTE of 17% or higher, but noted that the outlook for Hong Kong commercial real estate is “weak” due to “uncertain inflation and interest rate trends” (HKEJ 1, 2).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: HSBC’s miss reinforces the stagflationary tone of this macro regime — margin compression from lower rates and war-related credit losses hitting a bellwether for Asian trade and wealth flows.
  • Tactical Trade: Short HSI; PIVOT to neutral if ISM Services PMI prints above 54.0, signaling resilient US demand that could lift risk appetite.

docs.title: 📌 Strait of Hormuz: Fire on South Korean Ship, Seoul rebuffs Trump
docs.text:
Fact South Korea will investigate a fire on one of its vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, with President Trump blaming the incident on an Iranian attack (CNA). Seoul has rebuffed Trump’s call to join “Project Freedom,” indicating that “a UN mandate and a global coalition are required before Seoul joins Trump’s mission to reopen the strait” (SCMP). A US-flagged Maersk commercial vessel transited the strait under US military protection (BBC), which contributed to oil prices easing from their highs, though the IEA warned of a “dangerous escalation” (Al Jazeera).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Iran’s strikes on the UAE and a South Korean vessel harden the supply-risk premium, complicating the risk-on trade for Asian FX and supporting the case for elevated BOK hawkishness.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/KRW; PIVOT to short USD/KRW if South Korea announces it will join Project Freedom, signaling a de-escalation of bilateral risk.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• Australian household spending rose at its fastest pace in two years in March, driven by a spike in gasoline prices following the outbreak of war in Iran (BBG)
  • Philippine annual inflation accelerated to a three-year high of 7.2% in April, above the 5.5% median forecast, driven by a surge in fuel prices from the Middle East conflict (SCMP)
  • Indonesia’s economic growth accelerated to 5.6% in Q1, a three-year high, supported by a record increase in government spending (BBG)
  • Swiss inflation quickened to a 16-month high in April as energy costs jumped due to the war in the Middle East (BBG)
  • Singapore retail sales grew 4.8% in March, cooling from the Chinese New Year-driven surge in February (BT)
  • Indonesia’s central bank intervened in the FX markets as the rupiah set a new record low (BBG)
  • The Indian rupee fell to a record low as crude prices rose, with analysts dusting off the 2013 RBI playbook (BBG)

docs.text:
• RBA — Australia’s central bank raised its key interest rate by 25bp to 4.35%, delivering a third consecutive hike, with Governor Michele Bullock signaling policymakers would now pause to assess its next steps (BBG)
  • BSP — The Philippine central bank will likely need to take more aggressive interest-rate hikes to tame faster-than-expected inflation spurred by the Middle East war (BBG)

docs.text:
• Japan’s defence minister is touring Southeast Asia to sign defence pacts with Indonesia and the Philippines, aiming to turn them into “harder targets for Chinese maritime ambition” (SCMP)
  • The EU is sounding out industry groups over a new trade weapon to tackle Chinese overcapacity, with a proposal expected to be presented to Commission President Ursula von der Leyen on May 29 (SCMP)
  • Trump’s threats have given the EU pause for thought ahead of a US trade deal vote (FT)

docs.title: 2026 05 05 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 4 6PM to May 5 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

The US and Iran exchanged direct fire in the Strait of Hormuz, with Washington sinking Iranian fast boats and Tehran striking a UAE oil port and a South Korean-operated vessel, driving Brent above $115 and shattering the four‑week ceasefire. Shipping remains at a near‑standstill, and Kalshi prediction markets now see normal traffic resuming only in August or later. China invoked its anti‑sanctions law to defy US blacklisting of refiners, while Treasury Secretary Bessent pressed Beijing to help open the strait, raising the stakes ahead of the Xi‑Trump summit. ECB hawk Nagel reinforced the case for a June rate hike, and the EU braced for a threatened 25% US auto tariff. The cross‑market thread: risk‑off flight into oil and the dollar, while equities and risk‑sensitive Asian FX came under pressure.

docs.title: 📌 Strait of Hormuz: US‑Iran Live Fire Escalates, Shipping at Standstill
docs.text:
Fact The US military said it “sank Iranian small boats, shot down missiles and drones” as it began efforts to open the Strait of Hormuz, while Iran claimed it “prevented entry of US warships” into the waterway (Reuters 1, 2. Iran said its navy “forced a US warship to turn back” – a claim Washington denied (Al Jazeera). “An explosion and fire” struck a South Korean‑operated vessel anchored near the UAE inside the strait; Seoul reported no casualties among the 24 crew (SCMP). A fire broke out at the Fujairah petroleum complex in the UAE after an Iranian drone attack (Reuters). A US‑flagged Maersk vessel transited the strait accompanied by the US military, but most shipping remains at a standstill (Reuters, 2). Traders on Kalshi said traffic will not return to normal until August or later (CNBC). Brent crude jumped to $115 intraday on the flareup and held the bulk of the gain (BBG). S&P 500 slipped 0.1% from its all‑time high, the dollar strengthened, and bond yields rose (BBG).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The direct exchanges raise the probability of a sustained military standoff in the Strait, hardening the supply‑risk premium and reducing the credibility of the ceasefire.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to neutral if the next 48 hours produce no further kinetic engagement, signaling the risk premium was overshot.

docs.title: 📌 China Invokes Anti‑Sanctions Law, Bessent Pressures Beijing on Iran
docs.text:
Fact China invoked its anti‑sanctions law to counter US blacklisting of its refiners, ordering non‑compliance with US sanctions (Reuters). US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent urged China to “step up with some diplomacy and get the Iranians to open the strait,” noting that China buys “90 per cent of their energy” (SCMP). Bessent stressed the US has “absolute control” over the waterway even as he called on Beijing to pressure Tehran (Reuters). The dispute raises the temperature ahead of the Xi‑Trump summit, with US experts saying the Iran war could give Beijing the upper hand in those talks (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The legal showdown over sanctions increases pre‑summit tension and signals China will not yield on Iran ties, limiting US leverage.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/CNH on escalated bilateral risk; PIVOT to flat if the summit proceeds without additional punitive measures.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva warned of a “much worse outcome” if the Middle East war drags into 2027 (BusinessTimes).
  • EU Economy Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis warned of a stagflationary shock for the bloc from the Iran war (BBG).
  • US restaurant sales dropped as the Iran war pushed gasoline prices higher, a consumption‑led indicator (Reuters).
  • South African gasoline prices jumped to a near‑four‑year high as the government failed to fully offset higher oil prices (BBG).
  • Asian Development Bank’s chief economist urged countries to limit fuel subsidies to the most vulnerable, citing prolonged Middle East tensions straining public finances (BBG).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• Fed – New York Fed President John Williams said rates are “well positioned” amid war uncertainty and will need to be lower “at some point,” while former New York Fed President Bill Dudley backed Jerome Powell staying on after “merciless” attacks on the central bank (BBG).
  • ECB – Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel sees a case for a June rate hike without marked inflation progress (BBG). The ECB warned that weaker bank capital rules could fuel payouts, not lending (BBG).
  • South Africa – Central bank Governor Lesetja Kganyago said policymakers will “very carefully” monitor incoming data as the Iran war clouds the inflation outlook (BBG).
  • India – The government is exploring steps to mobilise US dollar inflows as the rupee slides, sources said (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• US President Donald Trump’s administration will move forward with plans to hike EU car tariffs, putting German carmakers in the crosshairs (Reuters). The EU vowed action but urged restraint (BBG).
  • The US, Gulf Arab nations drafted a new UN resolution on the Strait of Hormuz (Reuters).
  • The EU blocked funds for key Chinese solar energy parts, citing security concerns (FT).
  • India’s ruling BJP under Narendra Modi won a historic landslide in West Bengal, toppling the leftwing chief minister after 15 years (FT).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Oil held gains after the US‑Iran exchange, with Brent surging to $115 intraday (BBG).
  • Diamondback Energy, one of the biggest US shale producers, said it is boosting crude output immediately in response to rising prices (BBG).
  • Goldman Sachs warned that global oil stocks are approaching an eight‑year low and depletion speed is a concern (Reuters).
  • Chevron CEO warned that shortages in oil supply will begin appearing (Reuters).
  • Comex gold settled 2.38% lower at $4519.50, pressured by the stronger dollar (WSJ).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• South Korean stocks hit a fresh record, building on a historic monthly rally in April, despite regional risk‑off tone (CNBC).
  • US equity indexes (S&P 500, Dow) slipped from all‑time highs, driven by Middle East worries (Reuters).
  • European shares dropped 0.99%, with auto stocks under pressure from the tariff threat (HKEJ).

docs.title: 2026 05 06 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 6 8AM to May 6 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Hopes for an end to the Iran war surged intraday after Axios reported the US and Iran are closing in on a one‑page memo to halt hostilities, sending Brent briefly below $100 and triggering a powerful risk‑on sweep across all asset classes. The Kospi rocketed 6.5% to a new record above 7,000, propelled by Samsung’s market cap crossing $1 trillion on AI euphoria and the peace trade, while global bonds rallied as collapsing oil crushed rate‑hike bets. China’s services sector unexpectedly strengthened in April, and Hong Kong retail sales leaped 12.8%, reinforcing the regional resilience narrative even as US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon. The dominant cross‑market thread is the rapid unwinding of the Hormuz war premium on growing diplomatic conviction.

docs.title: 📌 US and Iran near one‑page peace memo; oil briefly slips below $100, risk assets soar
docs.text:
Fact US and Iran are closing in on a one‑page memo to end the war, Axios reported, citing a Pakistani source confirming the two sides are converging on the memorandum (Reuters 1, 2). Oil briefly dropped below $100 a barrel and gas slumped as Iran evaluated the new US proposal (BBG). Stocks and bonds rallied after the Axios report, with Asian shares higher and global bonds gaining as energy prices tumbled and rate‑hike expectations eased (Reuters 3, BBG 2). US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon for the first time since July 2022 as drivers faced continued strain from the protracted Middle East conflict (BBG 3).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The one‑page memo represents the most concrete de‑escalation signal yet, raising the probability of a near‑term Strait of Hormuz reopening and unwinding the energy supply shock that has driven global inflation and Fed tightening bets.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent; Long KRW and CNH on the risk‑on bid; Long US Treasuries as hawkish Fed repricing fades; PIVOT to neutral if Iranian officials reject the memo.

docs.title: 📌 Kospi closes up 6.5% as Samsung joins trillion‑dollar club on AI‑peace double‑boost
docs.text:
Fact South Korea’s Kospi surged to a new all‑time high, finishing the day up nearly 6.5%, breaking the 7,000 level (CNBC 1, HKEJ). Samsung Electronics’ market capitalisation surpassed $1 trillion as shares surged over 15% amid AI euphoria and peace‑driven relief for energy‑importing sectors (FT, CNBC 2). The rally was reinforced by a broad AI chip stock surge after AMD’s strong forecast and continued optimism around AI capital spending (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The extraordinary single‑day gain reflects a market aggressively pricing the peace dividend for Korean manufacturing inputs and the structural AI chip boom, reinforcing the Kospi’s status as the region’s AI/peace proxy.
  • Tactical Trade: Long KRW, Long Kospi; however, the pace of the rally leaves the index vulnerable to a sharp reversal if peace talks stall.

docs.title: 📌 China services PMI unexpectedly expands; Hong Kong retail sales jump 12.8%
docs.text:
Fact China’s services activity unexpectedly expanded at a stronger pace in April, with the Caixin/S&P Global services PMI rising to 51.9 from 51.7 in March, showing firms absorbing the Iran‑war price shock without passing on higher costs (BBG, SP Global). Hong Kong’s retail sales jumped by 12.8% year‑on‑year in March, bringing first‑quarter growth to 9.8%, driven by an 80.8% surge in vehicle sales ahead of tax‑break expirations (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Resilient services activity and consumption suggest that China’s domestic demand is weathering the energy shock better than feared, reducing downside risks to regional growth and easing pressure on Asia FX.
  • Tactical Trade: Long CNH, Long HKD as the macro‑stability premium builds; Long HSI for a consumer‑led re‑rating.

docs.title: 📌 Yen surges to two‑month high on intervention chatter; dollar drops across board
docs.text:
Fact The Japanese yen surged to over a two‑month high amid reports of fresh intervention by Japanese authorities, with the dollar dropping broadly as optimism grew for a US‑Iran deal (Reuters, Investing). The sharp move intensified chatter that the Ministry of Finance had stepped into the market, amplifying the broad dollar sell‑off on peace hopes.

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Apparent intervention, amplified by peace‑driven dollar weakness, is accelerating USD/JPY lower, which could compress import costs and reduce the urgency for BOJ tightening in the near term.
  • Tactical Trade: Short USD/JPY; PIVOT to flat if the Ministry of Finance confirms no intervention.

docs.title: 📌 Wang Yi calls for swift Hormuz reopening, backs Gulf‑led security framework
docs.text:
Fact Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi called for a swift reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and voiced support for a new regional security framework led by Gulf countries during talks with his Iranian counterpart (SCMP). Wang said China backed “the establishment of a regional peace and security framework led by regional countries” (SCMP). The meeting came a week before President Trump’s planned trip to Beijing (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Beijing is positioning as a credible diplomatic track to the US‑led process, potentially reducing the geopolitical risk premium on energy trade if a parallel framework yields concrete de‑escalation.
  • Tactical Trade: Long CNH on the geopolitical diversification tailwind; Short USD/Asia FX if the China‑backed framework gains traction.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• China’s Caixin/S&P Global services PMI unexpectedly rose to 51.9 in April from 51.7 in March, as firms absorbed the Iran‑war price shock without passing on higher costs to customers (BBG 1, SP Global).
  • Hong Kong retail sales jumped 12.8% y/y in March to HK$33.9 billion, driven by an 80.8% surge in car sales ahead of tax‑break expiry, taking Q1 growth to 9.8% (SCMP).
  • UK businesses raised prices at the fastest pace in over three years, imposing fuel surcharges as they sought to pass on soaring raw material and energy costs (BBG 2).
  • Oil shock pushed Thailand’s inflation to near the top end of the central bank’s target, ending a year‑long stretch of falling prices (BBG 3).
  • US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon for the first time since July 2022, nearing all‑time highs as drivers face persistent strain from the Middle East conflict (BBG 4).
  • ECB wage tracker indicates negotiated wage pressures stable in 2026, but the central bank projects pay growth will accelerate in the second half of the year (ECB, BBG 5).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• ECB — Euro‑area pay growth is set to quicken in the second half of the year, and the central bank is gauging whether spillovers from high energy costs will require it to increase interest rates (BBG).
  • Norges Bank & Riksbank — Both central banks are expected to keep rates steady this week, against contrasting inflation backdrops that starkly differ between the Nordic neighbours (BBG).
  • National Bank of Poland — The central bank is set to keep rates unchanged for a second month, with policymakers expected to sharpen anti‑inflation rhetoric as the Iran conflict revives long‑dormant price pressures (BBG).
  • Bank Indonesia — Indonesia tightened rules on foreign‑exchange purchases after the rupiah hit a new record low, in a bid to support the currency (WSJ, BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• Japan sent combat troops to the Philippines for the first time since World War II, participating in a ship‑sinking drill in the South China Sea, underscoring Tokyo’s growing willingness to project military power on China’s doorstep (BBG).
  • India and Vietnam agreed to strengthen economic and defence ties as both sides seek to deepen their partnership amid persistent Middle East tensions (BBG).
  • The EU’s push to bar Chinese suppliers from critical infrastructure under its proposed Cybersecurity Act would cost the bloc an estimated €367.8 billion over five years, including €146.2 billion to rip out and replace Chinese hardware (SCMP).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Oil briefly dropped below $100 a barrel and gas slumped as Iran evaluated a new US proposal to end the war, with Brent trading lower on optimism for a peace deal (BBG).
  • Grain and oilseed futures slid in Chicago on the peace optimism, with soy oil falling from a three‑year high after Trump’s Hormuz pause (BBG).
  • New Zealand is looking at storing fuel in Malaysia and Singapore as the impacts of the Iran war expose the risks from its limited domestic storage capacity (BBG).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• Asian stocks extended gains on peace hopes, with South Korea’s Kospi closing up nearly 6.5% to a record above 7,000 and the Hang Seng rising 315 points to reclaim 26,000 (WSJ, HKEJ).
  • Global bonds rallied, with the Gilts market leading gains as hopes for a US‑Iran deal curbed rate‑hike bets (BBG).
  • US chipmaker AMD’s strong AI‑fueled forecast sparked a broad rally in semiconductor stocks, adding fuel to the global tech advance (Reuters).

docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 5 6PM to May 6 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

Oil prices slumped after Trump paused the US “Project Freedom” Hormuz escort mission, citing “great progress” toward a final Iran deal. The announcement injected fresh de-escalation hopes even as the UAE reported its second consecutive day of missile and drone interceptions over Fujairah port. Markets seized on the diplomatic opening, sending the S&P 500 and Nasdaq to fresh records led by AI chip stocks, while US long bonds rebounded as yields fell back below 5%. South Korea’s inflation hit its fastest since July 2024 on surging energy costs, keeping pressure on the BOK to follow the RBA’s hawkish lead. The cross‑current remains the tug‑of‑war between the oil‑supply risk premium and the fragile ceasefire, with the Fed’s policy path now tilting toward a pre‑emptive rate hike if energy inflation proves sticky.

docs.title: 📌 Trump pauses Hormuz operation, cites Iran deal progress — oil drops but UAE attacked again
docs.text:
Fact US President Donald Trump said he is pausing “Project Freedom,” the US military’s bid to guide commercial ships out of the Strait of Hormuz, telling reporters, “Great Progress has been made on a final agreement to end the war with Iran” (Reuters 1, 2). US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the ceasefire with Iran remains in place despite growing tensions in the Strait (Al Jazeera). Hegseth later urged Iran “to be prudent” in its actions during the ceasefire (CNN). Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US has achieved the objectives of the Iran operation and described resolving the Hormuz situation as a test for the UN, urging against any Security Council veto (Reuters 3, 4). Meanwhile, the UAE said it came under Iranian missile and drone attacks for a second consecutive day, with air defence systems engaging the projectiles; Iran’s foreign ministry stated its forces “have not carried out any missile or drone operations against the UAE in recent days” (Reuters 5). The US Navy’s USS George H.W. Bush aircraft carrier was sailing in the Arabian Sea (US_CENTCOM). Oil fell more than $2 after the pause announcement (Reuters 6).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The pause reduces immediate military escalation risk, but the residual supply-risk premium persists as long as Iran and the UAE trade fire. A final deal could bring the Hormuz narrative to an end, crushing oil’s geopolitical bid and easing EM Asia’s energy‑import pain. Failure would harden the blockade and force a broad reassessment of global energy‑security architecture.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent on de‑escalation; PIVOT to long Brent if attacks on Fujairah escalate into a sustained disruption of UAE oil‑export capacity and the ceasefire collapses.

docs.title: 📌 Traders ramp up bets Warsh’s Fed could hike rates before cutting; job market stable
docs.text:
Fact Traders ramped up wagers that the Fed under incoming Chair Kevin Warsh could raise rates before cutting them (BBG). US job openings were little changed in March while hiring rebounded, showing a continued stabilisation of the labour market (BBG 2, Reuters). Fed Governor Michelle Bowman flagged consumer fraud could pose risks to the financial system (BBG 3). The US trade deficit widened in March as imports outpaced exports, signalling solid demand (BBG 4). The 30‑year Treasury yield fell back below 5% as buyers stepped in to lock in multi‑year highs (BBG 5).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A stable labour market and persistent inflation from the oil shock give the incoming Fed chair cover to hike, narrowing the window for Asian EM central banks to ease. That underpins a higher‑for‑longer global yield environment and a stronger dollar.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/Asia FX; Short KRW on the combination of rate‑hike expectations and accelerating domestic inflation.

docs.title: 📌 South Korea inflation hits fastest since July 2024, pressuring BOK
docs.text:
Fact South Korea’s consumer inflation picked up as surging energy costs began to feed more forcefully into the domestic economy, with price gains matching the fastest pace since July 2024 (BBG). The acceleration adds to headwinds for Asia EM currencies already under strain from the oil shock (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The BOK can no longer look through energy‑driven price pressure; a rate hike is becoming a tail‑risk scenario that would further widen the DXY‑KRW yield gap and accelerate capital outflows.
  • Tactical Trade: Short KRW; PIVOT to flat if the BOK explicitly pushes back against tightening and the May‑6 inflation print surprises to the downside.

docs.title: 2026 05 07 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 6 6PM to May 7 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

Optimism that Washington and Tehran are moving toward a one-page memorandum to end their two-month war ignited a global risk-on rotation, sending oil nearly 8% lower and propelling equities from Tokyo to New York to new highs. The AI investment cycle accelerated in parallel: AMD’s blowout data-center forecast drove its stock up 19%, Nvidia committed up to $3.2 billion to Corning’s optical-fiber buildout, and Anthropic struck a compute deal for SpaceX’s Colossus‑1 facility while revealing an 80‑fold first‑quarter growth rate, reinforcing chip‑capex euphoria. The yen surged abruptly to a ten‑week high on intervention chatter, adding a dramatic dollar‑weakness leg to the cross‑asset move, though the broad dollar sell‑off is equally attributable to peace‑driven demand for risk currencies. A fragile truce remains far from finalised while the Hormuz blockade is still being enforced, with a US fighter jet disabling an Iranian tanker even as China publicly urged a prompt reopening of the strait ahead of the Trump‑Xi summit next week.

docs.title: 📌 US‑Iran Peace Hopes Trigger Oil Collapse and Global Equity Surge
docs.text:
Fact Oil steadied after losing nearly 8% in the previous session, as the US and Iran weighed a fresh push to end the war in the Middle East (BBG 1). President Trump said the US had “very good talks” with Iran in the last 24 hours and that the war could end if Iran agrees to the proposal (Reuters 1, 2). Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi is in Beijing seeking support on a deal with the US and said the Islamic Republic is still reviewing Washington’s proposal (Al Jazeera). Emerging‑market stocks rallied to an all‑time high and a currency index rose to pre‑war levels as traders grew optimistic the two sides are moving closer to a peace deal (BBG 2). The S&P 500 and Nasdaq each notched record closes, with AMD’s results sparking an AI‑stock rally (Reuters). Japan’s Nikkei 225 hit an intraday record as markets reopened to catch up with the global equities rally (BBG 3). Brent crude briefly traded below $100 before steadying, while gold held a hefty 2.77% gain from its biggest daily advance since late March as truce hopes eased inflation fears (BBG 4). A US fighter jet disabled an Iranian oil tanker that was attempting to breach the American naval blockade of Iranian ports, US Central Command confirmed, underscoring that the blockade is still very much in force (SCMP, US_CENTCOM). Iran is simultaneously pushing ahead with a Hormuz toll “authority” despite reports of a deal (Lloyds_List). A one-page memorandum is now said to be close, but Iran has cautioned that it remains a US “wish list” until it is reality (CNN).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A framework agreement, even a non‑binding memorandum, would pull the tail‑risk of an open‑ended supply disruption, compressing energy‑cost fears globally and lowering the barrier for EM central banks to begin easing cycles.
  • Tactical Trade: Long EM equities and Short Brent; however, PIVOT to flat/neutral if Iran publicly rejects the memo this week, which would put the $100 floor back at risk.

docs.title: 📌 AI Capex Euphoria Intensifies: AMD, Nvidia/Corning, Anthropic Deals
docs.text:
Fact AMD shares hit a record high and surged 19% after the company forecast data‑center revenue well above estimates, with CEO Lisa Su explaining a massive forecast change driven by “unprecedented” AI infrastructure demand (CNBC 1, Reuters). Nvidia will invest up to $3.2 billion in Corning and partner on three new optical‑fiber factories in North Carolina and Texas focused on AI data‑center connectivity (CNBC 2). Anthropic has signed a deal to use all of the compute capacity at SpaceX’s Colossus‑1 data center in Memphis, Tennessee, and CEO Dario Amodei said the company’s first‑quarter growth was “80‑fold,” explaining its “difficulties with compute” (CNBC 3, WSJ). In China, DeepSeek is in talks to raise funds at a $50 billion valuation, and domestic chipmakers are racing to adopt its V4 large language model (WSJ 1, SCMP). The global chip rally also lifted Taiwan’s stock market, though the Hormuz crisis has exposed gaps in the island’s “silicon shield” because of its heavy dependence on seaborne energy supplies (BBG 5).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The simultaneous acceleration of hyperscale capex across CPUs, GPUs, optical interconnects, and AI‑cloud capacity signals that the AI investment super‑cycle is broadening, not peaking, supporting semi‑manufacturers and energy‑infrastructure plays.
  • Tactical Trade: Long KOSPI and Long TWD; PIVOT to flat if US restrictions on chip exports to China tighten abruptly after the Trump‑Xi summit, undercutting the DeepSeek‑fueled domestic AI narrative.

docs.title: 📌 Yen Surges Amid Intervention Speculation; Dollar Sinks on Peace Hopes
docs.text:
Fact The yen jumped abruptly to a ten‑week high, fueling fresh speculation that Japanese authorities may be intervening to stem dollar weakness, while the broader dollar slipped amid US‑Iran peace hopes (Investing_Forex 1, Reuters 2). Asian currencies strengthened, with the South Korean won outperforming after hot CPI data, as peace‑deal optimism accelerated the shift out of the dollar (Investing_Forex 2). The pound also jumped as the dollar sold off, with cable rising sharply on the combination of improved risk sentiment and speculation around Japanese intervention (Investing_Forex 3).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Yen volatility is being amplified by thin liquidity around Japan’s reopening, but the macro backdrop — falling oil, softer US yields, and peace hopes — independently supports a lower‑dollar regime that could sustain yen strength even without active intervention.
  • Tactical Trade: Long JPY vs USD; PIVOT to neutral if the MOF explicitly dismisses intervention this week, allowing the yen to retrace some of the speculative gains.

docs.title: 📌 China Presses Iran to Reopen Hormuz While US Trade Tensions Loom
docs.text:
Fact During a meeting between Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Iranian counterpart Abbas Araghchi in Beijing, China called for a “prompt resumption of shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz,” though that point was absent from Iran’s statement (CNBC 4). The meeting preceded next week’s Trump‑Xi summit, which will almost certainly include proclamations of cooperation but beneath the surface “major trade conflict is building,” according to trade experts (SCMP 2). Separately, the US trial of a man accused of operating a secret Chinese police station in New York has opened, adding to the bilateral friction (WSJ 2). The EU’s plan to phase out Chinese tech could cost the bloc over $400 billion, a Chinese study has found, illustrating the global decoupling headwind (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Beijing’s commercial urgency to get tankers moving again gives it a strong incentive to press Iran toward a deal, but the deeper trade rift with Washington suggests that any post‑peace risk‑on rally in CNH could be capped by tariff uncertainty.
  • Tactical Trade: Short CNH on a relative basis, expecting underperformance vs KRW and TWD if the summit fails to produce a tangible trade truce.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• US companies boosted payrolls by 109,000 in April, the most in over a year, though below the 120,000 median estimate in a Bloomberg survey (BBG, SCMP).
  • Sweden’s finance ministry expects the Nordic country’s economic recovery to be slowed more severely by the Iran war than previously thought, potentially denting the government’s re‑election prospects (BBG).
  • Egypt’s inflation unexpectedly slowed in April despite the Iran war spurring higher fuel costs and a weaker currency (BBG).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• Federal Reserve — Chicago Fed President Goolsbee warned that consumer behavior is showing signs of stress and that the central bank should not front‑run anticipated productivity gains by cutting rates prematurely (BBG 1, 2).
  • Bank of Canada — The Bank of Canada is weighing whether to spell out clearer guidance on how it responds to supply shocks in its monetary policy framework (BBG).
  • National Bank of Poland — Poland kept interest rates unchanged for a second month as the Iran war fallout on energy prices revives domestic inflation (BBG).
  • Bank Negara Malaysia — Malaysia is expected to keep its benchmark rate unchanged on Thursday as inflation remains benign despite higher global oil prices (BBG).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• Russia warned diplomatic missions to evacuate staff from Kyiv if Moscow launches a mass retaliatory strike, as the Russian military staged a huge attack on Ukraine on the eve of a proposed cease‑fire (Reuters, WSJ).
  • Israel struck Beirut for the first time since agreeing to a cease‑fire with Hezbollah last month, targeting a commander of the militant group’s elite Radwan force (Reuters).
  • US lawmakers in a rare push demanded transparency on Israel’s nuclear capability, arguing that the policy of ambiguity heightens risks amid the US‑Israel war on Iran (Al Jazeera).
  • France is moving its aircraft carrier to the Red Sea with an eye on a future Hormuz mission (Reuters).
  • The US is considering tapping oil under military bases to refill the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (BT).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• US crude and fuel inventories fell as the Iran war continues to roil energy markets, the EIA said, adding that the supply shock is expected to worsen even if the conflict ends (Reuters).
  • Fujairah oil product stocks fell nearly 7% to a new record low, underscoring acute product tightness at the Gulf bunkering hub (SP_Global).
  • Russian crude exports jumped 8% on sanctions relief, boosting global supply even as Hormuz‑related anxiety raises availability fears (SP_Global).
  • The Hormuz crisis slashed VLCC volumes by 36% but voyage lengths have lengthened as vessels reroute, raising freight costs (Lloyds_List).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite each closed at record highs, with strong US corporate earnings and AI optimism driving the advances (Reuters).
  • Japan’s Nikkei 225 surged to an intraday record high as markets reopened to catch up with the global equities rally (BBG).
  • European stocks rose more than 2% on the Stoxx 600, buoyed by both Iran deal optimism and positive earnings surprises (BT).
  • Emerging‑market stocks hit an all‑time high as the EM currency index returned to pre‑Iran‑war levels, reflecting broad risk appetite (BBG).

docs.title: 2026 05 08 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 8 8AM to May 8 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Ceasefire hopes suffered a fresh blow as the UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles, forcing Trump to insist the truce still holds while oil prices surged and Asian equities dropped. Trump set a July 4 deadline for the EU to ratify its trade deal or face “much higher” levies, and he invited Boeing and Mastercard CEOs to join his Beijing summit next week, injecting two‑way risk into the trade outlook. Prime Minister Starmer defied heavy local election losses and said he would stay on, triggering a relief rally in gilts and sterling. Japan’s wage data kept a June rate rise on the table as intervention speculation simmered. The cross‑currents harden a stagflationary impulse in which energy‑supply fear, trade brinkmanship, and political fragility converge on sustained volatility.

docs.title: 📌 UAE Intercepts Iranian Drones as Ceasefire Strain Deepens
docs.text:
Fact The UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles as tensions escalated in the Strait of Hormuz, even as Donald Trump insisted the ceasefire was still in effect (FT). The US military said it carried out retaliatory strikes against Iran after what it described as unprovoked attacks on its warships transiting the strait, while Iran accused the US of breaching the ceasefire and attacked American destroyers (Reuters). Brent crude rose sharply as both sides exchanged fire in the critical waterway (Al Jazeera). Trump said the ceasefire still holds but warned Iran to “sign the agreement fast” (Al Jazeera). The US also struck Iran’s Qeshm port and Bandar Abbas (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: UAE interceptions physically widen the conflict radius, making a near‑term reopening of Hormuz less credible and entrenching the oil‑supply risk premium that feeds into global inflation.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to flat/neutral if a verified US‑Iran de‑escalation communiqué and unimpeded Project Freedom convoy transit materialise within 24 hours.

docs.title: 📌 Trump Readies Slimmed‑Down CEO Delegation for Beijing Summit
docs.text:
Fact The Trump administration is inviting American CEOs from Boeing, Mastercard, Citigroup and other major corporations to join his delegation to China next week, according to multiple sources (SCMP_Econ). Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg said in April that the company was counting on the Trump administration to help unlock a long‑awaited major order from China (SCMP_Econ). The White House considered inviting about a dozen business leaders, compared with the 29 high‑profile executives on Trump’s last visit in 2017, Reuters reported (SCMP_Econ). Global soybean participants are awaiting the US‑China meeting with mixed expectations (SP_Global_Commodities). Trump overnight also gave the EU a July 4 deadline to ratify its trade agreement or face “much higher” tariffs (CNBC).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The China summit holds a binary catalyst for trade sentiment; a Boeing deal would signal that Sino‑US tensions are being managed despite the Iran war, while a failure would amplify protectionist fears and add to global growth headwinds.
  • Tactical Trade: Favours a tactical easing of CNH downside hedges ahead of the summit; PIVOT to add downside CNH protection if the EU rejects the July 4 deadline and the Beijing meeting fails to produce a concrete commercial outcome.

docs.title: 📌 Starmer Defies Heavy Losses, Gilts Rally on Political Survival
docs.text:
Fact UK government bonds rallied as Prime Minister Keir Starmer said he would stay as Labour leader despite the party’s heavy losses in local elections (BBG). Sterling also rose as Starmer vowed to fight on (Reuters). The populist Reform UK party made early gains, punishing Labour in former strongholds (Al Jazeera). The result deepens doubts over Starmer’s ability to govern but removes the immediate threat of a leadership vacuum (SCMP_Econ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Political continuity removes a near‑term tail risk for UK assets, but the surging Reform vote signals that fiscal populism and a harder line on trade could gain traction, complicating the medium‑term policy outlook.
  • Tactical Trade: Fading extreme gilt underperformance is warranted in the very short term; however, PIVOT to neutral if polling suggests Reform could emerge as a credible governing alternative.

docs.title: 📌 Japanese Wage Data Keeps June Hike in Play as Intervention Talk Swirls
docs.text:
Fact March wage growth in Japan undershot expectations but pressure to tighten is still on, supportive of a June rate rise (FT). Japan’s Ministry of Finance has intervened in the yen over the country’s Golden Week holiday, though markets are testing Tokyo’s resolve (CNBC). The service sector grew at the slowest pace in 11 months in April, according to the final au Jibun Bank PMI (BusinessTimes).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A June rate hike would put further upward pressure on JGB yields and narrow the rate gap with the US, providing fundamental support for the yen beyond intervention; a dovish tilt would trigger a fresh sell‑off.
  • Tactical Trade: Aggressively scaling into long JPY positions ahead of the June meeting once intervention‑driven volatility subsides.

docs.text:

  • Global food prices climbed to their highest level in more than three years as the Iran war disrupted supply chains (BBG).
  • The Philippines vowed a rapid ramp‑up in government spending starting this month to revive an economy hit by a sharp slowdown in growth and surging consumer prices (BBG).
  • Japan’s service sector growth slowed to an 11‑month low in April, with the au Jibun Bank Services PMI coming in at 52.2 (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 🌊 Macro Flux
docs.text:
May 1 12am to May 2 6pm HKT

Overview

The global monetary regime is pivoting from cut-bias to hike-bias as the Iran war's energy shock feeds through. The Fed's April FOMC statement signaled only cuts are on the table — three officials dissented, and the post-meeting narrative has shifted to "when might the Fed need to hike." The ECB and BOE are openly preparing June rate increases. Japan intervened with an estimated $34.5B to halt yen weakness. Oil touched $126/bbl before retreating. The cross-asset thread: the Iran war is compressing central bank optionality everywhere, forcing a synchronized hawkish repricing that will test equity valuations sustained by AI exuberance.

docs.title: 📌 Powell-Warsh Fed Clash Takes Shape as Three Dissenters Explain Votes
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (CNBC), (BBG), (BBG), (CNBC), (CNN), (WSJ)

The April FOMC decision to hold rates at 4.25-4.50% was unanimous, but the statement language — subtly signaling that only rate cuts are on the table — drew three dissents. Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack, and Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan all voted against the statement, arguing it pre-judged the next move in a highly uncertain environment. Kashkari and Hammack said the Iran war had fundamentally changed the rate outlook (BBG). The dissenters explicitly disagreed with hinting the next move would be a cut (CNBC). Kevin Warsh would face the risk of dissenting in his first FOMC vote at the June meeting (BBG). Powell has vowed he won't be a "shadow chair," but a Warsh clash will be tough to avoid (CNBC). The WSJ reported the Fed's internal discussion has shifted from "whether to cut" to "conditions for hiking" (WSJ).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Three FOMC dissents on a statement is a strong signal that the median FOMC is more hawkish than the Chair. The shift from cut-bias to hike-contingency compresses the Fed's reaction function — any upside inflation surprise from oil now triggers rate hike pricing rather than just a delay of cuts.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Supports short 2yr UST / flattening trades. Favors long USD vs low-yielders (EUR, JPY). Bearish for gold if real rates rise on hawkish repricing rather than inflation expectations alone.

docs.title: 📌 ECB and BOE Lean Toward June Rate Hikes on Iran Energy Shock
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (FT), (BBG), (BBG), (BBG), (BBG)

The ECB and BOE are both warning of rate rises as they grapple with the Middle East shock (FT). ECB's Nagel favors a June hike unless the outlook improves markedly (BBG). Rehn says fast ECB action is needed if second-round inflation is seen (BBG). Makhlouf says upside risks to inflation have intensified (BBG). BOE Governor Bailey says the central bank faces the "most difficult combination" of economic effects and cannot shield households from the Iran energy crisis (FT). The BOE's sole dissenter, Chief Economist Megan Greene, made the case for quick rate hikes, saying rates need to rise more promptly (BBG). If Warsh pushes for a US rate cut, it would go against the grain of G-7 action as soon as June (BBG).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — The ECB and BOE are being forced to hike into a growth slowdown — a classic stagflationary mix. This reduces the carry appeal of EUR and GBP versus USD, as the Fed retains more optionality to cut if growth deteriorates faster than inflation.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Favors short EUR/USD and short GBP/USD. Supports long EUR/USD vol given asymmetric risk to policy error.

docs.title: 📌 Japan Intervenes at $34.5B; Yen Spikes Then Fades
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBG), (Investing), (CNBC), (FT), (WallstreetCN)

Japan likely spent around $34.5B on its first currency intervention to prop up the yen since July 2024, according to Bloomberg analysis of central bank accounts (BBG). The yen spiked sharply on the intervention, wiping out Iran war losses, but weakened again the following day (CNBC). The Japanese Finance Minister had warned traders to keep their "cell phones close" ahead of the action (WallstreetCN). Citigroup exited yen trades after the intervention, citing oil risks (Investing). Market speculation of a second round of intervention emerged as the yen softened again (WallstreetCN). The FT reported Japan put the yen market on alert for Golden Week action (FT).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Intervention signals Japan's pain threshold is 160 USD/JPY. But with the BOJ still dovish and oil imports surging, fundamentals argue for further yen weakness. The $34.5B spent is roughly 0.6% of GDP — a credible signal but not a regime change unless backed by BOJ rate hikes.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Favors fading the intervention spike: short JPY crosses on rallies. The intervention creates a two-way risk for USD/JPY but does not change the fundamental direction.

docs.title: 📌 Oil Hits $126/bbl Then Retreats; Supply Crunch Nears
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (FT), (BBC), (FT), (CNBC), (Reuters)

Brent crude hit $126/bbl — the highest since 2022 — after a report that the US military would brief Trump on new Iran options, before falling back in volatile trading (FT, BBC). The oil market is one month from a crunch point as global reserves dwindle, with traders warning of a further jump in prices (FT). Exxon Mobil CEO said "the market hasn't seen the full impact" of the Iran war on oil prices (CNBC). Barclays lifted its 2026 Brent forecast to $100/bbl on prolonged Hormuz disruption (Reuters). The Strait of Hormuz blockade continues, with the US Treasury warning shippers not to pay tolls (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Oil at $126/bbl is a pure supply shock with no demand driver. This is stagflationary for all net importers (EU, Japan, India, China) and a windfall for net exporters (Saudi Arabia, Libya, Venezuela). The key variable is duration of the Hormuz closure — each month adds ~$5-10/bbl to structural price.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Favors long crude calendar spreads (backwardation). Supports short EUR/USD and short JPY on terms of trade deterioration. Favors long energy sector vs broad equity indices.

docs.title: 📌 Trump Escalates Trade War: 25% Tariff on EU Autos
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBC), (FT), (CNBC), (BBG)

Trump announced he will increase tariffs on EU cars and trucks to 25%, accusing the EU of "not complying with our fully agreed to trade deal" (BBC, FT). The tariffs will be imposed "next week" unless companies manufacture at American plants (FT). EU Parliament Trade Chief called the threat unacceptable (BBG). Separately, Trump removed Scotch whisky tariffs "in honor" of King Charles' state visit (FT, CNBC). UK goods exports to the US have not recovered from Trump's earlier tariffs, with the UK pushed into a trade deficit with its largest partner (FT, CNBC).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Auto tariffs target the EU's core export sector. This compounds the stagflationary shock from oil for the eurozone — weaker exports + higher energy costs = worse growth outlook. The whisky tariff removal is a diplomatic gesture with minimal macro impact.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Reinforces short EUR/USD view. Favors short European autos sector. Supports long USD on trade friction premium.

docs.title: 📌 US Sanctions China-Based Operator Over Iran Oil Trade; China UN Envoy Blasts US
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (SCMP), (SCMP)

The US sanctioned Qingdao Haiye Oil Terminal Co., Ltd and Chinese national Li Xinchun for importing "tens of millions of barrels of sanctioned Iranian crude oil" (SCMP). China's UN envoy blasted US "bullying" behavior as sanctions widened ahead of Trump-Xi talks (SCMP). The WSJ reported that China's "teapot" refiners offer a financial lifeline to Iran (WSJ).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Secondary sanctions on Chinese entities for Iran oil trade is a direct escalation point in US-China relations. This narrows the runway for a Trump-Xi deal and increases the risk of a broader trade/tech conflict. The "teapot" refiners are critical to Iran's oil revenue — targeting them raises the stakes.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Supports long oil on tighter Iran supply. Favors short CNH on geopolitical risk premium. Bearish for Chinese equities if sanctions broaden.

docs.title: 📌 China Extends Zero-Tariff Policy to 53 African Nations
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (SCMP), (BBC)

China scrapped tariffs for all African countries except Eswatini (which maintains ties with Taiwan) from May 1, covering 53 countries through April 30, 2028 (SCMP, BBC). The policy expands from 33 least-developed nations to include 20 middle-income African countries. CCTV reported the move as a "subtraction" that is also an "addition" for China-Africa trade (CCTV).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — This is a strategic move to deepen Africa supply chain integration at a time when the US is imposing tariffs on China. It secures access to African resources (rare earths, metals) and creates a counterweight to US-led decoupling. Modest near-term trade impact but significant for long-run supply chain reconfiguration.

docs.title: 📌 Apple Reports Strong China Revenue; iPhone Sales at Record
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (CNBC), (WallstreetCN)

Apple reported 17% revenue growth, topping estimates, with iPhone revenue at a record for the quarter. China revenue continued double-digit growth. CEO Tim Cook warned of extended memory/component constraints and said the company will "look at a range of options" (CNBC). Q3 guidance of 14-17% revenue growth topped analysts' estimates (CNBC). Apple execs plan to discuss China and tariffs on the earnings call but will avoid discussing the foldable iPhone, per Kalshi prediction markets (CNBC).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Apple's strong China revenue suggests consumer demand in China is holding up despite geopolitical tensions and sanctions rhetoric. The memory crunch warning is a supply-side constraint that could cap upside even with strong demand.

docs.title: 📌 Spirit Airlines Shuts Down as Iran War First Casualty
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBG), (FT), (Reuters)

Spirit Aviation Holdings wound down operations after surging fuel prices and a failed White House bailout (BBG, FT). Trump said the US would only proceed with a rescue "if it's a good deal" — it wasn't. Spirit is the first major corporate casualty of the Iran war (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Spirit's failure is a canary in the coal mine for high-beta, fuel-sensitive sectors. If oil stays above $100/bbl, expect more airline casualties and broader credit stress in transport/logistics.

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• Fed — April FOMC statement held rates at 4.25-4.50%. Three dissenters (Kashkari, Hammack, Logan) opposed statement language hinting at cuts. Internal discussion shifting to "conditions for hiking" per WSJ (WSJ). Warsh faces dissent risk in first vote at June meeting (BBG).
  • BOJ — No policy decision. Conducted estimated $34.5B FX intervention (BBG). Market on alert for Golden Week action (FT).
  • ECB — Lagarde signaled June hike possible. Nagel favors June hike unless outlook improves markedly. Rehn says fast action needed if second-round inflation seen. Makhlouf says upside risks to inflation intensified. Eurozone April inflation hit 3%.
  • BOE — Bailey says "most difficult combination" of economic effects. Chief economist Greene made case for quick rate hikes. BOE modeling points to higher rates this year.
  • RBA — Reuters poll: RBA to raise cash rate to 4.35% on May 5, with growing minority expecting further rises (Reuters).

docs.text:

docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 3 8AM to May 3 6PM HKT

Overview

The Iran war's commodity shock is the dominant macro driver this window, with Detroit carmakers warning of a $5bn cost hit, clean energy stocks drawing record inflows, and Middle East states scrambling for overland bypass routes around Hormuz. Taiwan's president defied China by landing in Eswatini, drawing Beijing's sharp condemnation, while the Philippines accused Chinese ships of illegal marine research in a fresh South China Sea flare-up. The connecting thread: supply-chain disruption — from oil logistics to rare earths to automotive inputs — is now the market's central pricing problem, reinforcing stagflationary tail risks across EM and DM assets.

docs.title: 📌 Commodity Shock: Iran War Disrupts Global Supply Chains
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (FT), (FT), (SCMP), (BBC) Detroit carmakers warned of a $5bn commodities shock due to the Iran war, per the FT, with the conflict now in its third month and causing a global energy shock on par with the 1970s oil crises (BBC). Investors are piling into clean power as the war drives a push for energy security, the FT reports. Middle Eastern governments are dusting off decades-old proposals for overland oil and gas pipelines and urgently drawing up plans for new rail-sea transport corridors to bypass the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea, according to the SCMP. Separately, the UK government has drawn up contingency plans allowing airlines to cancel flights weeks in advance without losing take-off and landing slots if they face fuel shortages this summer (BBC).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — The $5bn auto-sector cost is a leading indicator: margins compress, capex gets deferred, and the pass-through to core goods CPI is accelerating. The clean-power pivot is a structural bid, but it doesn't solve the immediate 3-6 month supply gap.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Supports long energy equities vs. consumer cyclicals. Favors long USD vs. EUR/GBP given Europe's higher energy import dependency. Middle East overland corridor talk is bullish for select EM infrastructure plays but too early for a trade.

docs.title: 📌 Taiwan/Eswatini: Diplomatic Clash with China
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (Reuters), (WSJ) Taiwan President Lai Ching-te began a trip to Eswatini, one of the few remaining diplomatic allies of Taiwan, drawing condemnation from China. China's foreign ministry called Lai a "rat" for the visit, per Reuters. The WSJ characterized the trip as Taiwan outfoxing China in a test of wills over the tiny African country.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — The Eswatini trip is symbolic but amplifies cross-strait tail risk at a time when the US is already stretched in the Middle East. Beijing's rhetorical escalation (calling a head of state a "rat") signals deeper frustration with Taiwan's diplomatic outreach.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Tilts risks toward short TWD/CNH. Any escalation beyond rhetoric would trigger a flight to USD/JPY and gold. For now, the market is pricing zero probability of kinetic action — this is the complacency that can break.

docs.title: 📌 South China Sea: Philippines Accuses China of Illegal Research
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (Bloomberg), (Reuters) The Philippine Coast Guard said four Chinese vessels were conducting illegal marine scientific research in its waters and threatened to deploy aircraft and boats to repel them, per Bloomberg. China and the Philippines traded accusations over the South China Sea, per Reuters.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — This is a recurring flashpoint, not a new escalation. The market has largely learned to ignore these incidents unless they involve direct vessel ramming or civilian casualties. The Philippines' threat to deploy aircraft is slightly more hawkish than the usual protest notes, but still within the normal deterrence playbook.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Negligible for broad macro. If the situation escalates to a naval collision, it would be a sharp risk-off event for Asia FX and equities. For now, this is background noise.

The flotilla of more than 50 vessels was intercepted by Israeli forces in international waters off Greece early on Thursday.

docs.title: 2026 05 03 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 2 6PM to May 3 8AM HKT

Overview

The Iran war remains the single dominant macro driver, with the Hormuz blockade continuing, oil prices elevated, and Trump reviewing a new 14-point Iranian proposal while threatening to restart strikes. The first major corporate casualty of the war emerged as Spirit Airlines shut down after a failed government bailout, a direct consequence of jet fuel prices doubling. OPEC+ provisionally agreed to another modest quota increase for June, while Beijing ordered Chinese firms to ignore US sanctions on refiners linked to Iranian oil. The week ahead opens with South Korea manufacturing PMI and a KTB auction, with markets focused on whether the Iran diplomatic track can deliver a breakthrough.

docs.title: 📌 Iran War: Trump Reviews New Proposal, Hormuz Blockade Continues
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (SCMP), (SCMP), (Reuters), (Reuters) President Trump said he was reviewing a new 14-point Iranian proposal delivered via Pakistan, responding to a 9-point US proposal. Trump expressed displeasure with the current state of negotiations and said the US could "restart strikes if they misbehave." A senior Iranian military officer said renewed fighting was "likely." The Hormuz blockade continues, with Iraq saying oil output and exports can recover within a week once the crisis ends. Separately, Senator Lindsey Graham (R-SC) said the US should "finish the job" if Iran does not yield (FT).

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — The diplomatic track remains fragile with both sides signaling willingness to continue fighting. The market is pricing a ceasefire premium in equities (S&P 500 extending weekly winning streak) but the underlying oil supply disruption remains unresolved.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Favors long oil and long defense/energy equities until there is a verified Hormuz reopening. Supports short duration on any headline suggesting diplomatic progress, as a ceasefire would release pent-up supply and steepen the curve.

docs.title: 📌 OPEC+ Agrees June Quota Increase
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBG) Major OPEC+ nations provisionally agreed to another modest, symbolic increase to supply targets for June, the group's first move since the UAE's surprise exit. The increase is modest and symbolic in scale.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — The quota increase is a political signal that OPEC+ still has spare capacity and is willing to use it, even as the Hormuz closure limits actual physical supply from the Gulf. The market impact is likely limited until the blockade lifts.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Neutral to slightly negative for oil prices near-term, but the real supply story remains the Hormuz chokepoint.

docs.title: 📌 Spirit Airlines Shuts Down — First Iran War Corporate Casualty
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBC), (SCMP), (Reuters) Spirit Airlines ceased operations after failing to secure a $500M government bailout from the Trump administration. The budget carrier was the first industry casualty of the Iran war, with jet fuel prices doubling over the two-month conflict. All flights were canceled, passengers stranded, and rival carriers scrambled to manage the disruption. Transportation Secretary Duffy said there is no need for a US budget airline bailout.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — Spirit's failure is a canary in the coal mine for sectors with high fuel cost exposure and thin margins. The transmission to broader markets is limited (Spirit was ~2% of US airline capacity), but the signal for credit markets is negative — the government declined to backstop a distressed company despite direct war-related cost shock.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Negative for high-yield credit in fuel-sensitive sectors. Supports long volatility in airline and transport credit.

docs.title: 📌 Beijing Orders Firms to Ignore US Sanctions on Iranian Oil Refiners
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (BBG) China ordered companies not to comply with US sanctions on five domestic refiners linked to the Iranian oil trade, deploying a blocking measure introduced in 2021 aimed at protecting firms from foreign laws deemed unjustified.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — This escalates the US-China dimension of the Iran conflict. The blocking statute creates legal ambiguity for Chinese firms operating in USD settlement systems, potentially accelerating de-dollarization efforts in oil trade. Direct risk of secondary sanctions on Chinese banks.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Bearish for CNH on sanctions escalation risk. Supports long gold as a reserve diversification trade. Favors long USD/CNH.

docs.title: 📌 S&P 500 Extends Longest Weekly Winning Streak Since Late 2024
docs.text:
Fact | Source: (WSJ) The S&P 500 extended its longest weekly winning streak since late 2024, with speculation that the worst of the Iran war is over driving a melt-up in AI and semiconductor stocks (BBG). Semiconductors rose in 21 of the last 23 sessions.

[AI Reasoning] Macro Lens — This is a classic "sell the rumor, buy the fact" reversal of the war-risk discount. The equity market is pricing a ceasefire that has not yet materialized, creating asymmetry. If diplomacy fails, the unwind could be violent.

[AI Reasoning] Potential Market Impact — Favors long VIX or tail hedges on US equities. The risk/reward for chasing the melt-up is poor given unresolved Iran risk.
