Shareholders rejected a workforce oversight report and approved say-on-pay (Reuters). Greg Abel took the stage at the annual meeting, making a case for the post-Buffett era (WSJ).

  • Spirit Airlines: Shut down after failing to secure a $500M government bailout. All flights canceled (BBC, CNBC, WSJ).

  • Nike: Slashed 1,400 more jobs as its stock crashed 70% from pandemic highs (Yahoo Finance).

  • Credit: Aegon Asset Management and Barclays are preparing for April's credit rally to potentially vanish (BBG). JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon issued a vague credit recession warning, but CNBC notes the bond market has more pressing issues (CNBC).

docs.title: 2026 05 04 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 4 8AM to May 4 7PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

The Hormuz crisis escalated further after Iran claimed a missile strike on a US warship entering the Strait — a claim denied by Washington — sending oil and defense stocks higher while European equities slid. China's unprecedented defiance of US sanctions was formalized, ordering non-compliance on five refiners, threatening a banking-sector showdown weeks before the Xi-Trump summit. The Bank of Korea signaled it's time to consider a rate hike, the first hawkish pivot from an Asian central bank since the war began, while ECB hawks doubled down on a June hike as eurozone inflation expectations ticked up. Across markets, Asian stocks outside Japan hit a record high on AI/tech earnings momentum, the euro held firm on rate expectations, and the yen spiked intraday on intervention fears, linking elevated geopolitical risk with divergent monetary policy paths.

docs.title: 📌 Hormuz Live Fire: Iran Claims Missile Strike on US Warship; Denied
docs.text:
Fact Iranian state media says two missiles have struck a US navy destroyer to prevent it entering the Strait of Hormuz (Al Jazeera). The US denied a report by Iranian media on Monday that the country had struck an American naval vessel with missiles, Axios reported citing a senior official (BBG). Oil prices and stocks pared back some of their earlier moves after the denial (BBG). Iran's Revolutionary Guards unveiled a new map of the Strait of Hormuz under their control, state media says (Reuters). Oil jumped after Iran's navy said it halted a US warship (Reuters). Japan's PM said the Hormuz closure is "inflicting an enormous impact" on Asia Pacific (Al Jazeera). A tanker was hit by unknown projectiles off UAE's Fujairah (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The unverified missile claim injects a binary headline risk premium; whether true or false, the threat of direct US-Iran naval engagement is now priced into oil and shipping.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to neutral if US provides conclusive evidence the claim is fabricated and Project Freedom proceeds without incident.

docs.title: 📌 China Defies US Sanctions on Oil Refiners Ahead of Trump Summit
docs.text:
Fact China has ordered its companies to ignore US sanctions, an unprecedented act of defiance that threatens to trap a vast banking sector in the crossfire as tension rises between the world's largest economies (BBG). The US action "violates international law and the basic norms of international relations", China's Ministry of Commerce said on Saturday (SCMP). The sanctions cover a refinery in Dalian owned by Shanghai-listed Hengli Petrochemical, along with four independent "teapot" refineries (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: China's formal defiance elevates financial decoupling from a tariff dispute to a direct sanctions-busting conflict, testing the Xi-Trump summit framework and risking secondary sanctions on Chinese banks.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/CNH on widening risk premium; PIVOT to flat if US Treasury provides a waiver or postponement before the summit.

docs.title: 📌 Bank of Korea Flags Readiness to Hike Rates
docs.text:
Fact Top Bank of Korea official says it's time to consider rate hike (BusinessTimes).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The BOK's hawkish signal breaks the dovish Asian central bank consensus, reflecting imported inflation pressure from energy costs and a strong dollar that is squeezing the current account.
  • Tactical Trade: Short USD/KRW on rate differential narrowing; PIVOT to long if the BOK delays beyond Q3 due to growth concerns.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text: • Euro-zone inflation will jump to 2.7% on average this year but return close to 2% next year, according to the ECB's quarterly survey of professional forecasters (BBG). Turkish annual inflation rose more than expected in April to 32.4%, as price pressures from the Iran war continued to ripple through the economy (BBG). Vietnam's inflation hit a six-year high and its trade gap reached a 30-year peak, squeezing the country's growth push (BusinessTimes). Indonesia's April inflation eased to 2.42% (BusinessTimes). A gauge measuring South African manufacturer sentiment rose in April to its best level in two years, potentially reflecting front-loading ahead of price increases due to the Iran war (BBG).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• ECB — It's "highly likely" that the ECB will have to raise interest rates at its next meeting in June, Governing Council member Peter Kazimir said (BBG). The ECB must be both cautious and ready to act on rates should inflation spread beyond a surge in oil prices, outgoing Governing Council member Francois Villeroy de Galhau said (BBG). Eurozone companies expect a new inflation surge if the war lasts months, an ECB poll showed (BusinessTimes).
  • Bank of Korea — Top Bank of Korea official says it's time to consider rate hike (BusinessTimes).
  • BOJ/MOF — A sudden surge in the yen ensured traders remained on edge over the potential for Japanese authorities to step back into the market after last week's intervention to curb declines (BBG). Goldman Sachs estimates Japan spent 5 trillion yen intervening last week and has capacity to intervene another 30 times (HKEJ).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text: • Europe is "ready to respond" should Trump follow through on his threat to raise tariffs on cars and trucks from the EU to 25%, according to Eurogroup President Kyriakos Pierrakakis (BBG). America's small businesses are being left out of Trump tariff refunds (FT). Singapore and New Zealand signed the world's first legally binding bilateral agreement to keep essential supplies flowing even during crises (SCMP, CNA). The US called Taiwan a "trusted and capable partner" and praised its ties with Eswatini (Reuters). An unfinished Iran war could give Xi the upper hand in Trump talks, sources told CNN (CNN).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text: • Oil steadied as traders questioned the workability of Trump's Plan to guide ships through Hormuz, with a tanker reportedly hit in the waterway (BBG). Oil then jumped after Iran's navy said it halted a US warship (Reuters). OPEC+ agreed a third oil output quota hike since the Hormuz closure, of 188,000 barrels per day (CNBC). Abu Dhabi's ADNOC plans to award $55 billion in projects through 2028 (WSJ). The UAE fertiliser giant Fertiglobe is resorting to trucks to shift product out of the Gulf, avoiding the Strait of Hormuz (FT). India resumed wheat exports after four years but high prices are likely to limit demand (Reuters).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text: • A rally in shares tied to AI helped Asia's stock benchmark outside Japan wipe out losses sparked by the Iran war and climb back to an all-time high (BBG). South Korea's KOSPI closed up 5.1% and Taiwan's TAIEX also hit a record high (HKEJ). European stocks tumbled as intensifying rhetoric between the US and Iran over the Strait of Hormuz pushed up oil prices (BBG). Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index rose 319 points to close above 26,000 in its May open (HKEJ). Bitcoin topped $80,000 for a three-month high as stocks rose (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 2026 05 04 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 3 6PM to May 4 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

The US confirmed Monday's "Project Freedom" military escort in the Strait of Hormuz, Iran called it a ceasefire violation, and two vessels were attacked – a bulk carrier off Sirik and a tanker off Fujairah – cementing a binary risk scenario for Brent and global shipping. Meanwhile, China activated its new "Blocking Rules" for the first time, ordering non-compliance with US sanctions on five oil refiners, a direct financial decoupling escalation that could test the Xi-Trump summit backdrop. The RBA is set to deliver a third straight rate hike (to 4.35%), cementing its hawkish outlier status against a backdrop of ECB recession warnings and a Fed constrained by Iran war uncertainty. Across markets, Asian stocks outside Japan are grinding toward all-time highs on AI/tech earnings optimism, while gold edges higher and oil steadies with a tanker-attack floor.

docs.title: 📌 Hormuz Escalation: "Project Freedom" launched, Iran warns of strikes, two vessels hit
docs.text:
Fact The United States will launch an effort on Monday to "guide" stranded ships from the Iran-gripped Strait of Hormuz, President Donald Trump said, as two ships around the strait reported attacks. Trump gave few details about what could be a sweeping attempt to help hundreds of vessels and some 20,000 seafarers. Iran quickly denounced the move as a ceasefire violation (SCMP). U.S. Central Command confirmed it will support the launch of Project Freedom in the Strait of Hormuz (US_CENTCOM). A tanker was hit by unknown projectiles off UAE's Fujairah, UKMTO says (Reuters 1). A bulk carrier was attacked by multiple small craft 11 nautical miles west of Sirik, Iran, according to UKMTO (Al Jazeera 1). An Iran lawmaker said the Strait of Hormuz will never return to the status quo that existed before the US-Israel war on Tehran (Al Jazeera 2). The Iranian military warned the US of attacks over the Hormuz mission (Al Jazeera 3). Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the US is "suffocating" Iran with economic and financial pressure (BBG 1). OPEC+ agreed a third oil output quota hike since the Hormuz closure (Reuters 2). Oil steadied as traders questioned the workability of Trump's plan, with a tanker reportedly hit in the waterway (BBG 2).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: "Project Freedom" triggers limited skirmishes but no full blockade breach, keeping Brent elevated near $85 on persistent risk premium.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent front-month calendar spread; PIVOT to flat if the first 24 hours conclude without any engagement, implying risk premium overdone.

docs.title: 📌 China Activates "Blocking Rules" for First Time Against US Sanctions on Oil Refiners
docs.text:
Fact China has ordered companies throughout the country not to comply with US sanctions on five Chinese oil refiners accused of trading in Iranian fuel, a move observers say could mark a new stage in Beijing's pushback against American long-arm jurisdiction. The order is the first application of a measure designed to block "improper" foreign actions, and could be a potential headache for US sanctions enforcement, according to analysts (SCMP 1). Beijing's decision to block Meta's $2 billion acquisition of Chinese AI start-up Manus was also described as a show of strength ahead of the Xi-Trump summit (SCMP 2). Separately, China's exports of telecoms, computer and information services reached 808 billion yuan ($118 billion) in 2025, up 13% YoY, far outpacing traditional goods (SCMP 3).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The "Blocking Rules" signal a new normal of selective financial decoupling, putting upward pressure on USD/CNH as capital flow risks rise.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/CNH via forwards or options; PIVOT to neutral if US refrains from immediate secondary sanctions, suggesting diplomatic space ahead of the summit.

docs.title: 📌 Central Bank Divergence: RBA Hawkish Outlier, ECB Recession Fears, Fed Hemmed In
docs.text:
Fact Australia's central bank is set to entrench its status as a hawkish outlier with a third straight interest-rate hike, diverging from peers that are mainly sitting tight to observe fallout from the US-Iran conflict (BBG 1). The RBA is expected to hike to 4.35% (BBG 2). ECB policymaker Stournaras said euro zone recession concerns are "real and justified" (Reuters 1). Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari said the Iran war limits the Fed's ability to provide rate guidance (Reuters 2). Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr warned that private credit could spark "psychological contagion" (BBG 3).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: RBA hike widens AUD/USD carry advantage short-term, but global risk-off from Hormuz caps upside; ECB recession and Fed pause reinforce USD strength vs EUR and JPY.
  • Tactical Trade: Short EUR/AUD on policy divergence; PIVOT to flat if Tuesday's RBA guidance sounds a markedly dovish note, signaling a peak.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• In 2025, China's exports of telecoms, computer and information services reached 808 billion yuan ($118 billion), marking a robust 13% year-on-year increase, far outpacing traditional goods like footwear which fell 9% to $46 billion (SCMP).
  • ECB policymaker Stournaras said euro zone recession concerns are "real and justified" (Reuters).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:

  • Fed — Minneapolis Fed President Kashkari said the Iran war limits the Fed's ability to provide rate guidance (Reuters 1). Fed Vice Chair Barr warned that private credit could spark "psychological contagion" (BBG 3).
  • ECB — ECB policymaker Stournaras said euro zone recession concerns are "real and justified" (Reuters 2).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• China ordered non-compliance with US sanctions on five oil refiners under new "Blocking Rules" — first application of the measure (SCMP).
  • North Korea and Russia discussed a rare five-year defence cooperation plan, which analysts say could accelerate Pyongyang's military modernisation and make China uneasy (SCMP).
  • German Chancellor Merz downplayed the rift with Washington after the US announced a drawdown of 5,000 troops based in Germany (Reuters 1), 2.
  • Israel approved a plan to buy F-35 and F-15IA fighter jets from Lockheed and Boeing (Reuters).
  • UK set to enter talks to join the EU's $105.9 billion Ukraine loan (Reuters).
  • UAE exited the Arab oil exporter group OAPEC (Reuters).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Oil steadied as traders questioned the workability of Trump's Hormuz plan, with a tanker reportedly hit in the waterway (BBG 1). OPEC+ agreed a third oil output quota hike since the Hormuz closure (Reuters 2).
  • Gold edged lower, following a second weekly decline, as traders monitored progress toward a US-Iran deal and Trump's Hormuz plan (BBG 2). Gold edges higher amid "signs of resilience" (WSJ).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• Stocks rallied, with Asian shares climbing toward all-time highs on optimism around the AI trade and stronger-than-expected earnings from megacap tech companies (BBG 1). The yen briefly spiked higher (BBG 2).
  • GameStop makes a bold $56 billion play for eBay, ready to go hostile (Reuters).
  • Foxconn launched second-generation satellites via SpaceX Falcon 9 (Reuters).

docs.title: 2026 05 05 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 5 8AM to May 5 7PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

The US said its ceasefire with Iran remains intact despite Monday’s direct‑fire exchange, but the operational reality is a stand‑off that keeps shipping clustered away from the Strait of Hormuz and oil supply risks elevated. The RBA delivered a third straight rate hike to 4.35% and signalled a pause, while Philippine inflation surged to 7.2% and the Indonesian rupiah and Indian rupee hit record lows, intensifying Asian EM stress. South Korea rebuffed Trump’s call to join the Hormuz mission, and China escalated its sanctions defiance as Washington threatened visa sanctions over illegal migration. HSBC shares tumbled 6% after a fraud‑linked charge dragged first‑quarter profit below estimates. The cross‑market thread is a risk‑off flight into the dollar, under pressure on EM FX, while oil holds elevated and global equities struggle for direction.

docs.title: 📌 Ceasefire Holds but Hormuz Stand‑Off Persists; Ships Cluster Further from Strait
docs.text:
Fact The US played down the prospect of a return to active war with Iran after a day of clashes in the Strait of Hormuz, stating that the ceasefire is still in place and the strikes were “all below the threshold” of restarting full combat (BBG, FT). Hundreds of vessels were seen clustering near Dubai as more ships moved away from the still‑empty strait, with Iran widening its area of control (BBG). Nine ships used the strait on Tuesday but “renewed tensions” are expected to slow traffic (SP_Global_Commodities). Shipping prepared for a potential Hormuz exit amid a confused US plan and renewed Iranian threats (Lloyds_List_Shipping). Brent eased on signs the US operation is loosening Iran’s blockade but held above $115 intraday (BT).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Washington’s down‑play of the clashes keeps the diplomatic path open but does not reverse the physical supply risk; shipping avoidance entrenches a persistent energy insurance premium.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent remains favoured, but PIVOT to flat if tanker traffic through the strait returns to pre‑clash levels for more than one day.

docs.title: 📌 South Korea Rebuffs Trump’s Project Freedom After Ship Fire
docs.text:
Fact Seoul indicated that a UN mandate and a global coalition are required before joining the US mission to reopen the strait, delivering a polite but firm no to Trump’s call that invoked a South Korean‑operated vessel hit by an explosion and fire (SCMP). Seoul is reviewing Trump’s navigation plan following the explosion on the Korean‑operated ship, with officials maintaining the need for a multinational framework (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: South Korea’s refusal signals alliance friction and raises the risk that other US allies hesitate, complicating any rapid reopening of the strait and prolonging supply disruptions.
  • Tactical Trade: Short KRW gains conviction; PIVOT neutral if Seoul signals a compromise on the UN mandate within the week.

docs.title: 📌 RBA Delivers Third Straight Hike, Signals Pause as Spending Surges
docs.text:
Fact Australia’s central bank raised its key interest rate to 4.35% from 4.1%, marking a third consecutive hike, with Governor Michele Bullock signalling policymakers would now pause to assess next steps (BBG). Australian household spending rose at its fastest pace in two years in March, driven by a spike in gasoline prices following the outbreak of the Iran war, complicating the RBA’s efforts to cool the economy (BBG). The RBA cited higher fuel prices from the war as a reason for the hike, cementing its outlier status among global peers (FT).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The surge in oil‑fuelled spending while the RBA pauses leaves Australian demand‑side inflation unchecked, risking a later forced hiking cycle if energy costs stay high.
  • Tactical Trade: Long AUD/NZD on policy divergence, but PIVOT flat if next month’s spending data show a rapid cooling that validates the pause.

docs.title: 📌 Asia EM FX Under Siege: PHP Inflation at 7.2%, IDR and INR Hit Record Lows
docs.text:
Fact Philippine inflation accelerated to 7.2% in April, the highest since March 2023, breaching the central bank’s forecast range and raising the chance of more aggressive rate hikes (BBG, SCMP). Indonesia’s central bank intervened as the rupiah set a new record low, with the governor previously warning the war in the Middle East would keep the currency under pressure (BBG). The Indian rupee fell to a record low, with analysts dusting off the playbook from the 2013 taper tantrum, as renewed US‑Iran tensions intensified economic worries (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Energy‑driven inflation forces more EM tightening, curbing growth just as external demand flags, increasing the risk of a broad EM sell‑off and forcing further FX intervention.
  • Tactical Trade: Short IDR and short INR, but PIVOT to long if oil retreats sustainably below $100 and BI/BSP/RBI clearly shift to easing mode.

docs.title: 📌 HSBC Shares Tumble 6% After Fraud‑Linked Charge Misses Profit
docs.text:
Fact HSBC reported first‑quarter pre‑tax profit of $9.4 billion, missing analysts’ estimates, as a surprise $400 million hit linked to a fraud‑related credit loss dragged on earnings, alongside provisions for Iran war risks (Reuters, SCMP). Shares slumped 6% in Hong Kong, dragging the Hang Seng Index back below the 26,000 level (Reuters, HKEJ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The fraud charge adds idiosyncratic risk to an already stretched Asia banking complex, potentially triggering a broader earnings downgrade cycle for lenders exposed to HK CRE and Iran‑war provisions.
  • Tactical Trade: Short HSI near‑term, but PIVOT long if HSBC’s CFO re‑states confidence and China trade data next week surprises to the upside.

# 🌊 Macro Flux May 4 6PM to May 5 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

The US and Iran exchanged direct fire in the Strait of Hormuz, with Washington sinking Iranian fast boats and Tehran striking a UAE oil port and a South Korean-operated vessel, driving Brent above $115 and shattering the four‑week ceasefire. Shipping remains at a near‑standstill, and Kalshi prediction markets now see normal traffic resuming only in August or later. China invoked its anti‑sanctions law to defy US blacklisting of refiners, while Treasury Secretary Bessent pressed Beijing to help open the strait, raising the stakes ahead of the Xi‑Trump summit. ECB hawk Nagel reinforced the case for a June rate hike, and the EU braced for a threatened 25% US auto tariff. The cross‑market thread: risk‑off flight into oil and the dollar, while equities and risk‑sensitive Asian FX came under pressure.

docs.title: 📌 Strait of Hormuz: US‑Iran Live Fire Escalates, Shipping at Standstill
docs.text:
Fact The US military said it “sank Iranian small boats, shot down missiles and drones” as it began efforts to open the Strait of Hormuz, while Iran claimed it “prevented entry of US warships” into the waterway (Reuters 1, 2). Iran said its navy “forced a US warship to turn back” – a claim Washington denied (Al Jazeera). “An explosion and fire” struck a South Korean‑operated vessel anchored near the UAE inside the strait; Seoul reported no casualties among the 24 crew (SCMP). A fire broke out at the Fujairah petroleum complex in the UAE after an Iranian drone attack (Reuters). A US‑flagged Maersk vessel transited the strait accompanied by the US military, but most shipping remains at a standstill (Reuters, 2). Traders on Kalshi said traffic will not return to normal until August or later (CNBC). Brent crude jumped to $115 intraday on the flareup and held the bulk of the gain (BBG). S&P 500 slipped 0.1% from its all‑time high, the dollar strengthened, and bond yields rose (BBG).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The direct exchanges raise the probability of a sustained military standoff in the Strait, hardening the supply‑risk premium and reducing the credibility of the ceasefire.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to neutral if the next 48 hours produce no further kinetic engagement, signaling the risk premium was overshot.

docs.title: 📌 China Invokes Anti‑Sanctions Law, Bessent Pressures Beijing on Iran
docs.text:
Fact China invoked its anti‑sanctions law to counter US blacklisting of its refiners, ordering non‑compliance with US sanctions (Reuters). US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent urged China to “step up with some diplomacy and get the Iranians to open the strait,” noting that China buys “90 per cent of their energy” (SCMP). Bessent stressed the US has “absolute control” over the waterway even as he called on Beijing to pressure Tehran (Reuters). The dispute raises the temperature ahead of the Xi‑Trump summit, with US experts saying the Iran war could give Beijing the upper hand in those talks (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The legal showdown over sanctions increases pre‑summit tension and signals China will not yield on Iran ties, limiting US leverage.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/CNH on escalated bilateral risk; PIVOT to flat if the summit proceeds without additional punitive measures.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva warned of a “much worse outcome” if the Middle East war drags into 2027 (BusinessTimes).
  • EU Economy Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis warned of a stagflationary shock for the bloc from the Iran war (BBG).
  • US restaurant sales dropped as the Iran war pushed gasoline prices higher, a consumption‑led indicator (Reuters).
  • South African gasoline prices jumped to a near‑four‑year high as the government failed to fully offset higher oil prices (BBG).
  • Asian Development Bank’s chief economist urged countries to limit fuel subsidies to the most vulnerable, citing prolonged Middle East tensions straining public finances (BBG).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• Fed – New York Fed President John Williams said rates are “well positioned” amid war uncertainty and will need to be lower “at some point,” while former New York Fed President Bill Dudley backed Jerome Powell staying on after “merciless” attacks on the central bank (BBG).
  • ECB – Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel sees a case for a June rate hike without marked inflation progress (BBG). The ECB warned that weaker bank capital rules could fuel payouts, not lending (BBG).
  • South Africa – Central bank Governor Lesetja Kganyago said policymakers will “very carefully” monitor incoming data as the Iran war clouds the inflation outlook (BBG).
  • India – The government is exploring steps to mobilise US dollar inflows as the rupee slides, sources said (BusinessTimes).

docs.text:
• US President Donald Trump’s administration will move forward with plans to hike EU car tariffs, putting German carmakers in the crosshairs (Reuters). The EU vowed action but urged restraint (BBG).
  • The US, Gulf Arab nations drafted a new UN resolution on the Strait of Hormuz (Reuters).
  • The EU blocked funds for key Chinese solar energy parts, citing security concerns (FT).
  • India’s ruling BJP under Narendra Modi won a historic landslide in West Bengal, toppling the leftwing chief minister after 15 years (FT).

docs.text:

  • Diamondback Energy, one of the biggest US shale producers, said it is boosting crude output immediately in response to rising prices (BBG).
  • Goldman Sachs warned that global oil stocks are approaching an eight‑year low and depletion speed is a concern (Reuters).
  • Chevron CEO warned that shortages in oil supply will begin appearing (Reuters).
  • Comex gold settled 2.38% lower at $4519.50, pressured by the stronger dollar (WSJ).

docs.text:

  • US equity indexes (S&P 500, Dow) slipped from all‑time highs, driven by Middle East worries (Reuters).
  • European shares dropped 0.99%, with auto stocks under pressure from the tariff threat (HKEJ).

docs.title: 2026 05 06 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 6 8AM to May 6 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Hopes for an end to the Iran war surged intraday after Axios reported the US and Iran are closing in on a one‑page memo to halt hostilities, sending Brent briefly below $100 and triggering a powerful risk‑on sweep across all asset classes. The Kospi rocketed 6.5% to a new record above 7,000, propelled by Samsung’s market cap crossing $1 trillion on AI euphoria and the peace trade, while global bonds rallied as collapsing oil crushed rate‑hike bets. China’s services sector unexpectedly strengthened in April, and Hong Kong retail sales leaped 12.8%, reinforcing the regional resilience narrative even as US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon. The dominant cross‑market thread is the rapid unwinding of the Hormuz war premium on growing diplomatic conviction.

docs.title: 📌 US and Iran near one‑page peace memo; oil briefly slips below $100, risk assets soar
docs.text:
Fact US and Iran are closing in on a one‑page memo to end the war, Axios reported, citing a Pakistani source confirming the two sides are converging on the memorandum (Reuters 1, 2). Oil briefly dropped below $100 a barrel and gas slumped as Iran evaluated the new US proposal (BBG). Stocks and bonds rallied after the Axios report, with Asian shares higher and global bonds gaining as energy prices tumbled and rate‑hike expectations eased (Reuters 3, BBG 2). US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon for the first time since July 2022 as drivers faced continued strain from the protracted Middle East conflict (BBG 3).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The one‑page memo represents the most concrete de‑escalation signal yet, raising the probability of a near‑term Strait of Hormuz reopening and unwinding the energy supply shock that has driven global inflation and Fed tightening bets.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent; Long KRW and CNH on the risk‑on bid; Long US Treasuries as hawkish Fed repricing fades; PIVOT to neutral if Iranian officials reject the memo.

docs.title: 📌 Kospi closes up 6.5% as Samsung joins trillion‑dollar club on AI‑peace double‑boost
docs.text:
Fact South Korea’s Kospi surged to a new all‑time high, finishing the day up nearly 6.5%, breaking the 7,000 level (CNBC 1, HKEJ). Samsung Electronics’ market capitalisation surpassed $1 trillion as shares surged over 15% amid AI euphoria and peace‑driven relief for energy‑importing sectors (FT, CNBC 2). The rally was reinforced by a broad AI chip stock surge after AMD’s strong forecast and continued optimism around AI capital spending (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The extraordinary single‑day gain reflects a market aggressively pricing the peace dividend for Korean manufacturing inputs and the structural AI chip boom, reinforcing the Kospi’s status as the region’s AI/peace proxy.
  • Tactical Trade: Long KRW, Long Kospi; however, the pace of the rally leaves the index vulnerable to a sharp reversal if peace talks stall.

docs.title: 📌 China services PMI unexpectedly expands; Hong Kong retail sales jump 12.8%
docs.text:
Fact China’s services activity unexpectedly expanded at a stronger pace in April, with the Caixin/S&P Global services PMI rising to 51.9 from 51.7 in March, showing firms absorbing the Iran‑war price shock without passing on higher costs (BBG, SP Global). Hong Kong’s retail sales jumped by 12.8% year‑on‑year in March, bringing first‑quarter growth to 9.8%, driven by an 80.8% surge in vehicle sales ahead of tax‑break expirations (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Resilient services activity and consumption suggest that China’s domestic demand is weathering the energy shock better than feared, reducing downside risks to regional growth and easing pressure on Asia FX.
  • Tactical Trade: Long CNH, Long HKD as the macro‑stability premium builds; Long HSI for a consumer‑led re‑rating.

docs.title: 📌 Yen surges to two‑month high on intervention chatter; dollar drops across board
docs.text:
Fact The Japanese yen surged to over a two‑month high amid reports of fresh intervention by Japanese authorities, with the dollar dropping broadly as optimism grew for a US‑Iran deal (Reuters, Investing). The sharp move intensified chatter that the Ministry of Finance had stepped into the market, amplifying the broad dollar sell‑off on peace hopes.

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Apparent intervention, amplified by peace‑driven dollar weakness, is accelerating USD/JPY lower, which could compress import costs and reduce the urgency for BOJ tightening in the near term.
  • Tactical Trade: Short USD/JPY; PIVOT to flat if the Ministry of Finance confirms no intervention.

docs.title: 📌 Wang Yi calls for swift Hormuz reopening, backs Gulf‑led security framework
docs.text:
Fact Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi called for a swift reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and voiced support for a new regional security framework led by Gulf countries during talks with his Iranian counterpart (SCMP). Wang said China backed “the establishment of a regional peace and security framework led by regional countries” (SCMP). The meeting came a week before President Trump’s planned trip to Beijing (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Beijing is positioning as a credible diplomatic track to the US‑led process, potentially reducing the geopolitical risk premium on energy trade if a parallel framework yields concrete de‑escalation.
  • Tactical Trade: Long CNH on the geopolitical diversification tailwind; Short USD/Asia FX if the China‑backed framework gains traction.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• China’s Caixin/S&P Global services PMI unexpectedly rose to 51.9 in April from 51.7 in March, as firms absorbed the Iran‑war price shock without passing on higher costs to customers (BBG 1, SP Global).
  • Hong Kong retail sales jumped 12.8% y/y in March to HK$33.9 billion, driven by an 80.8% surge in car sales ahead of tax‑break expiry, taking Q1 growth to 9.8% (SCMP).
  • UK businesses raised prices at the fastest pace in over three years, imposing fuel surcharges as they sought to pass on soaring raw material and energy costs (BBG 2).
  • Oil shock pushed Thailand’s inflation to near the top end of the central bank’s target, ending a year‑long stretch of falling prices (BBG 3).
  • US gasoline prices topped $4.50 per gallon for the first time since July 2022, nearing all‑time highs as drivers face persistent strain from the Middle East conflict (BBG 4).
  • ECB wage tracker indicates negotiated wage pressures stable in 2026, but the central bank projects pay growth will accelerate in the second half of the year (ECB, BBG 5).

docs.text:
• ECB — Euro‑area pay growth is set to quicken in the second half of the year, and the central bank is gauging whether spillovers from high energy costs will require it to increase interest rates (BBG).

docs.title: 2026 05 06 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 5 6PM to May 6 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

Oil prices slumped after Trump paused the US “Project Freedom” Hormuz escort mission, citing “great progress” toward a final Iran deal. The announcement injected fresh de-escalation hopes even as the UAE reported its second consecutive day of missile and drone interceptions over Fujairah port. Markets seized on the diplomatic opening, sending the S&P 500 and Nasdaq to fresh records led by AI chip stocks, while US long bonds rebounded as yields fell back below 5%. South Korea’s inflation hit its fastest since July 2024 on surging energy costs, keeping pressure on the BOK to follow the RBA’s hawkish lead. The cross‑current remains the tug‑of‑war between the oil‑supply risk premium and the fragile ceasefire, with the Fed’s policy path now tilting toward a pre‑emptive rate hike if energy inflation proves sticky.

docs.title: 📌 Trump pauses Hormuz operation, cites Iran deal progress — oil drops but UAE attacked again
docs.text:
Fact US President Donald Trump said he is pausing “Project Freedom,” the US military’s bid to guide commercial ships out of the Strait of Hormuz, telling reporters, “Great Progress has been made on a final agreement to end the war with Iran” (Reuters 1, 2). US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the ceasefire with Iran remains in place despite growing tensions in the Strait (Al Jazeera). Hegseth later urged Iran “to be prudent” in its actions during the ceasefire (CNN). Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US has achieved the objectives of the Iran operation and described resolving the Hormuz situation as a test for the UN, urging against any Security Council veto (Reuters 3, 4). Meanwhile, the UAE said it came under Iranian missile and drone attacks for a second consecutive day, with air defence systems engaging the projectiles; Iran’s foreign ministry stated its forces “have not carried out any missile or drone operations against the UAE in recent days” (Reuters 5). The US Navy’s USS George H.W. Bush aircraft carrier was sailing in the Arabian Sea (US_CENTCOM). Oil fell more than $2 after the pause announcement (Reuters 6).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The pause reduces immediate military escalation risk, but the residual supply-risk premium persists as long as Iran and the UAE trade fire. A final deal could bring the Hormuz narrative to an end, crushing oil’s geopolitical bid and easing EM Asia’s energy‑import pain. Failure would harden the blockade and force a broad reassessment of global energy‑security architecture.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent on de‑escalation; PIVOT to long Brent if attacks on Fujairah escalate into a sustained disruption of UAE oil‑export capacity and the ceasefire collapses.

docs.title: 📌 Traders ramp up bets Warsh’s Fed could hike rates before cutting; job market stable
docs.text:
Fact Traders ramped up wagers that the Fed under incoming Chair Kevin Warsh could raise rates before cutting them (BBG). US job openings were little changed in March while hiring rebounded, showing a continued stabilisation of the labour market (BBG 2, Reuters). Fed Governor Michelle Bowman flagged consumer fraud could pose risks to the financial system (BBG 3). The US trade deficit widened in March as imports outpaced exports, signalling solid demand (BBG 4). The 30‑year Treasury yield fell back below 5% as buyers stepped in to lock in multi‑year highs (BBG 5).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A stable labour market and persistent inflation from the oil shock give the incoming Fed chair cover to hike, narrowing the window for Asian EM central banks to ease. That underpins a higher‑for‑longer global yield environment and a stronger dollar.
  • Tactical Trade: Long USD/Asia FX; Short KRW on the combination of rate‑hike expectations and accelerating domestic inflation.

docs.title: 📌 South Korea inflation hits fastest since July 2024, pressuring BOK
docs.text:
Fact South Korea’s consumer inflation picked up as surging energy costs began to feed more forcefully into the domestic economy, with price gains matching the fastest pace since July 2024 (BBG). The acceleration adds to headwinds for Asia EM currencies already under strain from the oil shock (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The BOK can no longer look through energy‑driven price pressure; a rate hike is becoming a tail‑risk scenario that would further widen the DXY‑KRW yield gap and accelerate capital outflows.
  • Tactical Trade: Short KRW; PIVOT to flat if the BOK explicitly pushes back against tightening and the May‑6 inflation print surprises to the downside.

docs.title: 2026 05 07 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 7 8AM to May 7 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Asian equities surged to fresh records and the dollar weakened as optimism over a potential US‑Iran peace deal pushed oil prices sharply lower, though the rally was tempered by the EU’s failure to finalize a US trade pact—risking new tariffs on cars—and China’s decision to invoke its anti‑sanctions law while asking banks to halt loans to US‑sanctioned refiners. The Nikkei 225 soared past 62,000 for the first time and Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index jumped more than 400 points, with chip names leading the charge, as traders looked past President Trump’s warning that Iran would be bombed at a “much higher level” if it rejects the peace proposal. Oil’s retreat below $100 a barrel and the broad risk‑on rotation are strengthening the case for a near‑term peace dividend, but the twin headwinds of transatlantic trade friction and US‑China sanctions escalation prevent a clean break. The cross‑market thread remains oil‑price volatility, with peace hopes simultaneously lifting risk assets and easing inflation fears while fresh trade and sanction tensions inject geopolitical uncertainty.

docs.title: 📌 Risk‑On Rally Extends as Oil Drops on Peace Hopes
docs.text:
Fact Asian stocks hit a record high and the dollar fell on Thursday as oil prices continued to pull back on optimism over a US‑Iran peace deal (Reuters) Japan’s Nikkei 225 surged over 5% to a record high, topping 62,000 for the first time, after markets reopened from Golden Week holidays (CNBC) Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index climbed more than 400 points, with chip stocks leading the advance (HKEJ) Brent crude fell further as investors bet on a potential reopening of the Strait of Hormuz (WSJ) President Donald Trump said the conflict in the Middle East would be “over quickly” if Iran agreed to the peace proposal, while Iran was reviewing a one‑page peace plan drawn up by the United States (SCMP)

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The peace narrative acts as the dominant macro force, fueling a classic risk‑on rotation, but the absence of a formal Iranian response keeps oil volatility elevated and prevents a decisive break in sentiment.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Asia ex‑Japan equities favored on peace meme; PIVOT to flat if Iran’s official response rejects the proposal, sending oil back above $105.

docs.title: 📌 EU Fails to Finalize US Trade Deal, Risks Fresh Tariffs
docs.text:
Fact The European Union failed to finalize a long‑delayed US trade deal during overnight talks, despite warnings from President Donald Trump that he would soon impose fresh tariffs on EU cars (BBG) The impasse raises the risk of a new trade front that could dent European exports and amplify the war‑induced supply‑chain strains

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The collapse of US‑EU trade talks injects a fresh layer of uncertainty for European manufacturing, complicating the ECB’s policy outlook and accelerating a pivot toward defensive trade measures.
  • Tactical Trade: Short EUR/USD on tariff risk premium; PIVOT to long if a last‑minute deal materializes ahead of the May 9 deadline.

docs.title: 📌 China Invokes Anti‑Sanctions Law, Halts Loans to Sanctioned Refiners
docs.text:
Fact Beijing ordered the blocking of US sanctions on five oil refineries, marking the first invocation of its 2021 anti‑sanctions law (Al Jazeera) Separately, China asked its banks to pause new loans to US‑sanctioned Chinese refiners, according to Bloomberg News (Reuters, BusinessTimes) The moves signal a hardening of China’s response to US pressure ahead of next week’s Trump‑Xi summit

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: China’s invocation of its anti‑sanctions law represents an escalation in the economic cold war, raising the probability of a rift at the summit and encouraging a de‑risking of supply chains linked to Chinese refiners.
  • Tactical Trade: Short CNY proxy (KRW, TWD) on sanctions contagion fear; PIVOT to neutral if the Trump‑Xi meeting yields a sanctions relief agreement.

docs.title: 📌 Yen Intervention Follow‑Up Falls Flat as Markets Test Resolve
docs.text:
Fact Japanese authorities likely used another $30 billion in follow‑up intervention in the currency market just days after an earlier round of action, according to a Bloomberg analysis of central bank accounts (BBG) The yen weakened on Thursday as traders questioned the effectiveness of the interventions, with the Nikkei 225 surging over 5% to a record, partly on a softer yen (CNBC) The Ministry of Finance’s intervention during the Golden Week holiday has left the market questioning the durability of the support amid a wide rate gap with the US (CNBC)

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The yen intervention is proving ineffective as the dovish BOJ stance and wide interest‑rate differential encourage carry trades, forcing Tokyo to consider additional measures, including possible coordination with the US Treasury during Bessent’s visit.
  • Tactical Trade: Short JPY favored if the BOJ does not shift policy; PIVOT to long JPY if Bessent’s Japan visit yields a joint intervention commitment.

  • Indonesia’s economy grew 5.6% during the first quarter of 2026, its fastest pace in more than three years, but analysts warn that headwinds loom from the Iran conflict (SCMP)
  • UK construction slump worsened in April as cost pressures intensified, with the S&P Global UK Construction PMI posting the largest decline in output since November (BBG)

  • Bank Negara Malaysia — Bank Negara Malaysia held its benchmark interest rate steady at 3.0% for a fifth straight meeting, warning over risks to growth from the prolonged Middle East conflict (BBG)
  • ECB — ECB should keep its options open for the June meeting, said departing French central bank governor François Villeroy de Galhau, amid uncertainty from the Iran war and trade tensions (FT)

docs.title: 2026 05 07 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 6 6PM to May 7 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

Hopes that the US and Iran could strike a peace deal to end the two‑month war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz sent oil prices tumbling below $100 a barrel and sparked a global equity rally, though a US airstrike disabling an Iranian tanker, Iran’s push for a strait toll authority, and a lack of concrete surrender terms injected caution, leaving risk assets swinging on diplomatic headlines. The S&P 500 touched record territory on the back of stunning corporate earnings, but Fed officials warned that mounting supply‑chain disruptions could entrench inflation, complicating the rate outlook. With a Trump‑Xi summit next week, simmering US‑China trade tensions added a second front of geopolitical uncertainty for investors, cementing oil volatility as the dominant cross‑market thread.

docs.title: 📌 US‑Iran Peace Negotiations & Strait of Hormuz Dynamics
docs.text:
Fact Iran is reviewing a US proposal to end the war, though key demands remain unaddressed (CNA). President Donald Trump warned that Iran will be bombed at a ‘much higher level’ if it doesn’t agree to the peace deal (CNBC). China called for a ‘prompt resumption of shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz’ ahead of the Trump‑Xi summit (CNBC). Iran said it will ensure the safe passage of ships through Hormuz (CCTV). However, Iran is pressing ahead with a toll ‘authority’ for the strait (Lloyd's List). A US fighter jet disabled an Iranian oil tanker in the Gulf of Oman that tried to breach the American naval blockade (US_CENTCOM, SCMP). France moved its aircraft carrier to the Red Sea with an eye on a potential Hormuz mission (Reuters). Trump also said the United States will get uranium from Iran (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The peace push is genuine but fragile, with conflicting signals—official review, continued blockade enforcement, Iranian toll authority, and allied military posturing—suggesting that even a formal ceasefire won’t quickly reopen Hormuz, keeping energy risk premiums elevated.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent on near‑term de‑escalation momentum; PIVOT to long if a deal collapses or fails to include immediate strait reopening.

docs.title: 📌 Oil Price Plunge & Supply Shock
docs.text:
Fact Brent crude futures fell to $97 a barrel before rebounding to above $101 after reports of a potential US‑Iran deal (BBC). US crude and fuel inventories fell again last week, and further declines are expected even if the conflict ends, according to EIA data (Reuters). Oil product stocks at Fujairah fell nearly 7% to a new record low (S&P Global). US fuel exports hit an all‑time record as the Hormuz closure spurred demand from Asia and Europe (FT). The US is considering tapping oil under military bases to refill its strategic petroleum reserve (Business Times). Russian crude exports jumped 8% on sanctions relief, adding supply pressure (S&P Global).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: De‑escalation hopes temporarily collapsed the war premium, but the physical supply chain is so degraded that even a political solution won’t quickly refill inventories, capping downside for crude.
  • Tactical Trade: Short Brent to harvest a peace premium fade, but PIVOT to long if the ceasefire does not include a clear timeline for commercial Hormuz transit.

docs.title: 📌 Equity Rally Wavers on Deal Uncertainty
docs.text:
Fact A rally that drove global stocks to all‑time highs wavered as traders awaited updates on the US‑Iran peace deal (BBG). Europe’s Stoxx 600 closed up over 2% on Iran deal optimism (Business Times). The S&P 500 and Nasdaq edged higher as crude oil fell ahead of the update (Yahoo Finance). US corporate profit strength ignited the charge to record peaks (Reuters). Apollo CEO Rowan warned of elevated risk of unexpected shocks and said he is positioning defensively for an expected correction (CNBC).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Peace‑driven disinflation and strong earnings are fueling a risk‑on melt‑up, but the rally’s pause on deal uncertainty signals limited upside unless a verifiable ceasefire is completed.
  • Tactical Trade: Long US equities into a deal announcement, take profits if negotiations stall; layer in hedges if oil spikes back above $110.

docs.title: 📌 Fed Officials Warn on Supply‑Chain Inflation
docs.text:
Fact Fed officials say rising supply chain risks fuel concern that inflation may prove more persistent than expected (Reuters). The San Francisco Fed’s advisory council reported that businesses are increasingly uncertain and the economic outlook is deteriorating, though consumer spending is holding up (SF_Fed). Kraft Heinz’s CEO said consumers are ‘literally running out of money’ (WSJ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Supply‑driven inflation risks, even if the war ends, will keep the Fed cautious, supporting a higher‑for‑longer rate environment and tempering dovish bets.
  • Tactical Trade: Short front‑end Treasuries (bear flattening) on sticky inflation; long USD/JPY as the Fed remains firm while BOJ intervention may prove limited.

docs.title: 2026 05 08 afternoon
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 8 8AM to May 8 6PM HKT Good afternoon.

Overview

Ceasefire hopes suffered a fresh blow as the UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles, forcing Trump to insist the truce still holds while oil prices surged and Asian equities dropped. Trump set a July 4 deadline for the EU to ratify its trade deal or face “much higher” levies, and he invited Boeing and Mastercard CEOs to join his Beijing summit next week, injecting two‑way risk into the trade outlook. Prime Minister Starmer defied heavy local election losses and said he would stay on, triggering a relief rally in gilts and sterling. Japan’s wage data kept a June rate rise on the table as intervention speculation simmered. The cross‑currents harden a stagflationary impulse in which energy‑supply fear, trade brinkmanship, and political fragility converge on sustained volatility.

docs.title: 📌 UAE Intercepts Iranian Drones as Ceasefire Strain Deepens
docs.text:
Fact The UAE intercepted Iranian drones and missiles as tensions escalated in the Strait of Hormuz, even as Donald Trump insisted the ceasefire was still in effect (FT). The US military said it carried out retaliatory strikes against Iran after what it described as unprovoked attacks on its warships transiting the strait, while Iran accused the US of breaching the ceasefire and attacked American destroyers (Reuters). Brent crude rose sharply as both sides exchanged fire in the critical waterway (Al Jazeera). Trump said the ceasefire still holds but warned Iran to “sign the agreement fast” (Al Jazeera). The US also struck Iran’s Qeshm port and Bandar Abbas (Reuters).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: UAE interceptions physically widen the conflict radius, making a near‑term reopening of Hormuz less credible and entrenching the oil‑supply risk premium that feeds into global inflation.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to flat/neutral if a verified US‑Iran de‑escalation communiqué and unimpeded Project Freedom convoy transit materialise within 24 hours.

docs.title: 📌 Trump Readies Slimmed‑Down CEO Delegation for Beijing Summit
docs.text:
Fact The Trump administration is inviting American CEOs from Boeing, Mastercard, Citigroup and other major corporations to join his delegation to China next week, according to multiple sources (SCMP_Econ). Boeing CEO Kelly Ortberg said in April that the company was counting on the Trump administration to help unlock a long‑awaited major order from China (SCMP_Econ). The White House considered inviting about a dozen business leaders, compared with the 29 high‑profile executives on Trump’s last visit in 2017, Reuters reported (SCMP_Econ). Global soybean participants are awaiting the US‑China meeting with mixed expectations (SP_Global_Commodities). Trump overnight also gave the EU a July 4 deadline to ratify its trade agreement or face “much higher” tariffs (CNBC).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The China summit holds a binary catalyst for trade sentiment; a Boeing deal would signal that Sino‑US tensions are being managed despite the Iran war, while a failure would amplify protectionist fears and add to global growth headwinds.
  • Tactical Trade: Favours a tactical easing of CNH downside hedges ahead of the summit; PIVOT to add downside CNH protection if the EU rejects the July 4 deadline and the Beijing meeting fails to produce a concrete commercial outcome.

docs.title: 📌 Starmer Defies Heavy Losses, Gilts Rally on Political Survival
docs.text:
Fact UK government bonds rallied as Prime Minister Keir Starmer said he would stay as Labour leader despite the party’s heavy losses in local elections (BBG). Sterling also rose as Starmer vowed to fight on (Reuters). The populist Reform UK party made early gains, punishing Labour in former strongholds (Al Jazeera). The result deepens doubts over Starmer’s ability to govern but removes the immediate threat of a leadership vacuum (SCMP_Econ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: Political continuity removes a near‑term tail risk for UK assets, but the surging Reform vote signals that fiscal populism and a harder line on trade could gain traction, complicating the medium‑term policy outlook.
  • Tactical Trade: Fading extreme gilt underperformance is warranted in the very short term; however, PIVOT to neutral if polling suggests Reform could emerge as a credible governing alternative.

docs.title: 📌 Japanese Wage Data Keeps June Hike in Play as Intervention Talk Swirls
docs.text:
Fact March wage growth in Japan undershot expectations but pressure to tighten is still on, supportive of a June rate rise (FT). Japan’s Ministry of Finance has intervened in the yen over the country’s Golden Week holiday, though markets are testing Tokyo’s resolve (CNBC). The service sector grew at the slowest pace in 11 months in April, according to the final au Jibun Bank PMI (BusinessTimes).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: A June rate hike would put further upward pressure on JGB yields and narrow the rate gap with the US, providing fundamental support for the yen beyond intervention; a dovish tilt would trigger a fresh sell‑off.
  • Tactical Trade: Aggressively scaling into long JPY positions ahead of the June meeting once intervention‑driven volatility subsides.

docs.text:

  • Global food prices climbed to their highest level in more than three years as the Iran war disrupted supply chains (BBG).
  • The Philippines vowed a rapid ramp‑up in government spending starting this month to revive an economy hit by a sharp slowdown in growth and surging consumer prices (BBG).
  • Japan’s service sector growth slowed to an 11‑month low in April, with the au Jibun Bank Services PMI coming in at 52.2 (BusinessTimes).

docs.title: 2026 05 08 morning
docs.text:
# 🌊 Macro Flux May 7 6PM to May 8 8AM HKT Good morning.

Overview

The US-Iran ceasefire was severely tested overnight as both sides exchanged fire in the Strait of Hormuz, sending crude oil sharply higher and re‑injecting a supply‑disruption risk premium into global markets. A US trade court simultaneously struck down Trump’s 10% global tariff as illegal, while the president set a July 4 deadline for the EU to ratify a trade deal or face “much higher” levies. Within the Fed, three senior officials publicly aired dissent over the rate‑path signal, and ECB’s Schnabel warned rate hikes may be needed if the energy shock broadens. The cross‑currents reinforce a stagflationary impulse: conflict‑driven oil spikes collide with fractured central bank guidance and escalating tariff uncertainty.

docs.title: 📌 US‑Iran Ceasefire Frays as Forces Exchange Fire in Hormuz
docs.text:
Fact Oil jumped following renewed clashes between US and Iranian forces, jeopardizing the outlook for a deal to end the 10‑week war (BBG). The US military said it carried out retaliatory strikes against Iran after what it described as unprovoked attacks on its warships transiting the Strait of Hormuz (Reuters). Gulf states lifted restrictions that had blocked “Project Freedom” in the Strait of Hormuz (WSJ). Iran’s military said it attacked US Navy ships after they targeted an Iranian tanker, and state media reported explosions near Bandar Abbas (Al Jazeera). The US also struck Iran’s Qeshm port and Bandar Abbas (Reuters). A Chinese‑owned oil tanker was hit near the Strait while the US paused a ship‑protection plan (Reuters). Neither side said the ceasefire was formally dead, though both accused the other of violations (Al Jazeera).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The exchange of fire materially raises the probability of a formal ceasefire collapse and prolongs the Strait’s effective closure, sustaining elevated energy costs and reinforcing global inflationary pressures.
  • Tactical Trade: Long Brent; PIVOT to flat/neutral if a high‑level bilateral agreement is announced within the next 24 hours and the US‑escorted “Project Freedom” begins without further kinetic contact.

docs.title: 📌 US Trade Court Rules 10% Global Tariff Illegal, Issues Narrow Block
docs.text:
Fact A US trade court ruled on Thursday that President Donald Trump’s 10% global tariffs are illegal but issued only a narrow block affecting the specific businesses that sued (Reuters). The ruling represents a setback to the administration’s trade policy but could be appealed (WSJ). The small‑business plaintiffs had argued the tariffs exceeded presidential authority (WSJ).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The ruling puts a legal ceiling on broad tariff escalation, reducing the immediate risk of a universal import levy that would stoke consumer inflation and disrupt supply chains.
  • Tactical Trade: Short USD/KRW on reduced trade‑war tail risk; PIVOT to flat if the administration announces an immediate appeal that extends the legal uncertainty.

docs.title: 📌 Trump Sets July 4 Deadline for EU Trade Deal or “Much Higher” Tariffs
docs.text:
Fact President Donald Trump said he told EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen the bloc must ratify a trade deal by July 4 or “their Tariffs would immediately jump to much higher levels” (BBC). The July 4 deadline ties the tariff threat to the US 250th birthday (Reuters). European officials had previously fallen short of agreement on the pact (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The explicit deadline raises the odds of a transatlantic trade disruption that would hit European exports and global risk sentiment, especially if the EU struggles to reach internal consensus.
  • Tactical Trade: Short EUR/USD on tariff‑risk‑driven euro underperformance, PIVOT to neutral if this week’s US‑China summit yields a major trade breakthrough that reduces protectionist momentum.

docs.title: 📌 FOMC Dissent Widens; ECB Warns of Energy‑Shock Rate Hike
docs.text:
Fact Boston Fed President Susan Collins favored changing the FOMC statement language along with the two voting members who dissented (BBG). Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said the FOMC’s signal that interest rates could be cut soon was misleading given inflation risks from the war (BBG). San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly played down divisions, signaling she would not have dissented as some colleagues did (BBG). Separately, ECB Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel said the central bank would need to hike if the oil‑price shock broadened into broader inflation expectations and wages (BBG).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The public dissent erodes the FOMC’s forward‑guidance credibility, raising the risk of a hawkish repricing once the war‑driven uncertainty recedes. Schnabel’s warning signals the ECB is on alert for a policy tightening if energy prices sustainably lift core inflation.
  • Tactical Trade: Short US rates (favor higher front‑end yields) on breakdown of dovish consensus; PIVOT to neutral if Friday’s US payrolls print strongly amid a ceasefire breakthrough.

docs.title: 📌 Trump’s China Summit On Track; CEO Delegation Invited
docs.text:
Fact President Donald Trump is forging ahead with plans to hold a summit with President Xi Jinping next week in Beijing, despite unease among Chinese officials about holding the meeting before the war in Iran is resolved (BBG). The White House is inviting CEOs from Nvidia, Apple, Boeing, Exxon, Qualcomm, Blackstone, Citigroup, and Visa to accompany the president (SCMP). China’s Premier Li Qiang told visiting US senator Steve Daines that Beijing hoped to maintain “stable and predictable” trade ties (SCMP).

[AI Reasoning]

  • Base Case: The summit remains a potential de‑escalation catalyst, with a large corporate delegation suggesting a possible aircraft or energy deal that could ease trade tensions and provide China with a symbolic win.
  • Tactical Trade: Long CNH into the summit on the prospect of a positive‑sum outcome; PIVOT to short if Chinese media hardens its Iran‑war rhetoric and signals deep displeasure with Washington.

docs.title: 📊 Economic Indicators
docs.text:
• US productivity growth cooled in Q1 as hours worked rebounded, though it still rose (BBG).
  • US consumer borrowing increased in March by the most since late 2022, reflecting a surge in non‑revolving credit and higher card balances (BBG).
  • US jobless claims rose last week (WSJ).
  • Japan’s real wages rose for a third consecutive month in March, supporting the BOJ’s case for further rate hikes (BBG).
  • Germany expects a €52‑billion ($58.9bn) federal tax‑revenue hole through 2030, blaming the Iran war (BBG).
  • US short‑term inflation expectations inched up in April, according to the New York Fed Survey (BBG).
  • Whirlpool said the Iran war caused a “recession‑level industry decline” in the US as consumer confidence collapsed (CNBC).

docs.title: 🏦 Central Banks
docs.text:
• Fed — Boston Fed President Collins favored changing the post‑meeting statement along with the two dissenters (BBG). Cleveland Fed Hammack called the rate‑cut signal misleading given war‑fueled inflation risks (BBG). San Francisco Fed’s Daly said statement language is less important than action (BBG).
  • ECB — Executive Board member Schnabel said the ECB will need to hike if the energy shock broadens into wages and inflation expectations (BBG).
  • Banxico — Cut its benchmark rate by 25 bp to 6.5% and declared its two‑year easing cycle over (BBG).
  • MNB — Hungary’s vice governor signaled a rate cut as early as June could be on the cards, citing the forint’s post‑election rally (BBG).

docs.title: 🌐 Geopolitics & Policy
docs.text:
• The US sanctioned an Iraqi oil ministry official and Iran‑linked militias for aiding Iranian oil sales (WSJ).
  • The US expanded Cuba sanctions to a nickel joint venture tied to Canadian miner Sherritt, with implications for China‑linked battery supply chains (SCMP).
  • China sentenced two former defence ministers, Wei Fenghe and Li Shangfu, to death with a two‑year reprieve for corruption (WSJ).
  • A UK court convicted two men linked to the Hong Kong trade office of spying on activists for China (FT).
  • Thailand and Cambodia pledged to pursue trust‑building measures and enforce a fragile ceasefire after last year’s deadly border fighting (SCMP).
  • Ukraine struck deep inside Russia while Moscow doubled down on threats to strike Kyiv ahead of the May 9 Victory Day parade (WSJ).

docs.title: 🛢️ Commodities
docs.text:
• Brent crude jumped following renewed US‑Iran hostilities, jeopardising peace efforts (BBG). Brent futures rose $2.69 to $105.78 a barrel (Reuters).
  • Comex gold settled 0.38% higher at $4,699.80 as truce hopes dimmed (WSJ).
  • Poland’s central bank plans to increase its gold reserves by 105 tonnes (WallstreetCN).
  • HKEX plans to relaunch gold futures in coming months to capture growing mainland Chinese demand (SCMP).
  • Saudi Arabia boosted logistics spending to blunt the impact of the Iran war and Strait closure, adding to its Q1 budget deficit (BBG).

docs.title: 📈 Equities
docs.text:
• Asian stocks pulled back from a record high as escalating tensions revived oil‑supply fears (BBG).
  • The S&P 500 ended lower as chip stocks gave up gains (Reuters).
  • The pan‑European Stoxx 600 closed in the red, reversing morning gains, as investors assessed Middle East peace prospects (CNBC).
  • Nvidia will invest up to $2.1 billion in IREN as part of an AI data‑center deal, fueling a continued AI equity bid (CNBC).
